legal-foundations.definition: The legal authority where your business is registered and subject to its laws
legal-foundations.example: A legal jurisdiction for a start-up is like the "home country or city" whose rules and laws the business has to follow. Learn more about Owl Explains for additional insights.

legal-foundations.definition: A legal business structure that provides limited liability protection while allowing for investment from unlimited shareholders
legal-foundations.example: Most venture-backed startups choose Delaware C-Corps for their established legal precedents and investor familiarity

legal-foundations.definition: The regulatory categorization of a digital token as either a utility (functional tool) or security (investment)
legal-foundations.example: A token that provides immediate access to platform services without promising returns is more likely to be classified as a utility

legal-foundations.definition: A four-part test used by the SEC to determine if a transaction qualifies as an investment contract (security)
legal-foundations.example: If users buy tokens primarily expecting profit from the efforts of the development team, the token likely qualifies as a security under the Howey Test (see: https://www.investopedia.com/terms/h/howey-test.asp)

legal-foundations.definition: Regulatory requirements to verify customer identities and prevent financial crimes
legal-foundations.example: Using a third-party compliance platform to automatically screen users against sanctions lists and verify their identities before allowing large transactions

security-fundamentals.definition: Controlling who has access to what resources within your organization
security-fundamentals.example: Implementing a role-based access matrix that removes all access credentials during employee offboarding

security-fundamentals.definition: The process of moving code from development to production with appropriate security controls
security-fundamentals.example: Setting up a pipeline that requires security testing and multiple approvals before code can be deployed to production

security-fundamentals.definition: A physical device that safeguards and manages digital keys and performs encryption functions
security-fundamentals.example: Using an HSM to store the private keys for your project's treasury wallet, keeping the keys offline and protected from unauthorized access

security-fundamentals.definition: Information stored directly on a blockchain, making it immutable and publicly verifiable
security-fundamentals.example: Storing NFT ownership records on-chain to ensure transparency and censorship resistance

security-fundamentals.definition: A process requiring explicit approval for accessing elevated privileges
security-fundamentals.example: Requiring multiple team members to approve access to admin functions through a time-limited permission system

business-model-canvas.definition: A strategic management template that helps founders articulate their core assumptions about target audience, value propositions, and sustainability strategies
business-model-canvas.example: A DeFi protocol using the canvas to map out how they'll provide value to liquidity providers while generating sustainable revenue

business-model-canvas.definition: An expanded view of who interacts with your product, including token holders, community members, followers, and contributors
business-model-canvas.example: A DAO considering not just active governance participants but also passive token holders as part of their customer base

business-model-canvas.definition: The methods through which Web3 projects generate and retain value, including tokenomics, treasury strategies, and yield approaches
business-model-canvas.example: A GameFi project using a combination of NFT sales, in-game currency, and revenue sharing with content creators

business-model-canvas.definition: The economic system governing a token's creation, distribution, and utility within a project's ecosystem
business-model-canvas.example: A protocol designing token incentives that reward both early adopters and long-term contributors to ensure sustainable growth

user-personas.definition: A detailed profile of your ideal user that outlines their needs, behaviors, pain points, and daily workflows
user-personas.example: A developer at a Series B startup with Solidity background who needs better tools for testing smart contracts

user-personas.definition: Potential users without existing relationships to founders who provide more honest, unbiased feedback
user-personas.example: A participant found through a Discord community rather than through personal connections

user-personas.definition: The systematic process of collecting user input, implementing changes, and showing results to encourage more feedback
user-personas.example: Tracking how many users report a specific UI issue, fixing it, and highlighting the improvement in release notes

user-personas.definition: Blockchain data that reveals user behavior patterns and can inform persona development
user-personas.example: Segmenting users by transaction frequency, wallet age, or staking behavior

grants-process.definition: Unlike VCs seeking ROI, foundation grants prioritize ecosystem impact
grants-process.example: A project creating open-source infrastructure that many can build upon

grants-process.definition: How well your project addresses a specific gap or need in the Avalanche ecosystem
grants-process.example: Building privacy solutions that enable real-world business transactions on Avalanche

grants-process.definition: Evidence that demonstrates your ability to execute and deliver on your roadmap
grants-process.example: A working prototype on Fuji testnet that shows core functionality

grants-process.definition: Applying for grants across multiple ecosystems without customization or commitment
grants-process.example: Submitting identical proposals to Avalanche, Solana, and Ethereum without blockchain-specific considerations

grants-process.definition: Connections and introductions provided by the foundation to help your project grow
grants-process.example: Getting introduced to institutional custodians that would be difficult to reach independently

go-to-market.definition: A detailed description of the type of company that would derive the most value from your product and provide value to your business
go-to-market.example: An identity solution startup might define their ICP as 'government agencies in developing regions seeking to implement national digital ID systems'

go-to-market.definition: A comprehensive plan that outlines how a company will reach target customers and achieve competitive advantage
go-to-market.example: A structured approach that identifies target companies, defines personas, leverages networks, and builds replicable success stories

go-to-market.definition: Using blockchain as infrastructure without being token-based, bridging traditional and Web3 technologies
go-to-market.example: A digital identity solution that uses blockchain for secure verification but interfaces with traditional systems without requiring token interactions

go-to-market.definition: Specific roles or individuals within target companies who influence or make purchasing decisions
go-to-market.example: Technical directors who evaluate blockchain integration feasibility, or CEOs concerned with regulatory compliance

go-to-market.definition: Strategic engagement with developer and user communities that influence adoption decisions
go-to-market.example: Creating educational resources for developer communities that demonstrate how your solution integrates with existing systems

sales-mastery.definition: The core emotional benefit your product provides to customers
sales-mastery.example: "We create new revenue streams" vs. "We offer a blockchain loyalty platform"

sales-mastery.definition: Detailed profile of decision-makers who will purchase your product
sales-mastery.example: CMO at mid-sized retail brand concerned with customer retention

sales-mastery.definition: Early adopter who collaborates on product development rather than expecting perfection
sales-mastery.example: A brand willing to run a 3-month pilot with weekly feedback sessions

sales-mastery.definition: Measurable outcome that demonstrates your product's value
sales-mastery.example: "15% increase in repeat purchases after implementing our loyalty program"
Introduction → Agenda → Customer pain points → Product overview → Q&A

sales-mastery.definition: Tool for tracking leads and automating follow-ups
sales-mastery.example: Using HubSpot to schedule reminder emails for prospects who haven't responded

pricing-strategies.definition: Combines a fixed subscription fee with variable usage charges
pricing-strategies.example: $1599/month base fee plus additional costs based on storage usage

pricing-strategies.definition: The direct costs associated with providing your Web3 service
pricing-strategies.example: Gas fees, validator costs, cloud infrastructure, and personnel expenses

pricing-strategies.definition: Charging clients based on their actual consumption of resources
pricing-strategies.example: Charging per transaction, API call, or data storage amount

pricing-strategies.definition: Building in margin for strategic discounts while maintaining profit expectations
pricing-strategies.example: Setting enterprise list price at $5000/month with room to offer up to 20% discount during negotiations

pricing-strategies.definition: Focusing on the business value delivered rather than unit costs
pricing-strategies.example: Highlighting $2M in potential savings rather than discussing the $50K service cost

community-building.term: Supply and Demand in Social Media
community-building.definition: Early-stage projects lack established demand and social capital
community-building.example: Instead of posting daily without purpose, focus on creating one highly valuable, educational post per week

community-building.definition: Breaking long-form content into multiple smaller formats
community-building.example: Converting a one-hour livestream into promotional clips, articles, social threads, and quote graphics

community-building.definition: Community involves active participation while audience merely observes
community-building.example: 100 active members discussing your project daily is more valuable than 10,000 silent followers

community-building.definition: Testing content ideas on a small scale before expanding
community-building.example: Avalanche tested a simple 'Triangle adoption' post to gauge community response before launching a full campaign

community-building.definition: A one-page document outlining an event's goals, audience, and success metrics
community-building.example: Creating a document specifying that a hackathon aims to generate 10 new project prototypes from senior developers

fundraising-fundamentals.definition: The process of moving potential investors from initial awareness to committed capital, similar to a sales pipeline
fundraising-fundamentals.example: A founder starts with 100 cold outreach emails, gets 20 intro calls, 5 follow-up discussions, and ultimately 2 term sheets

fundraising-fundamentals.definition: A centralized repository containing all due diligence materials an investor might need to evaluate your company
fundraising-fundamentals.example: A Notion-based data room containing company formation documents, financial projections, team backgrounds, and product roadmaps

fundraising-fundamentals.definition: The primary investor who typically sets the terms and often takes a board seat in a funding round
fundraising-fundamentals.example: A venture capital firm commits to 50% of your funding round and helps bring in other investors to complete the round

fundraising-fundamentals.definition: Regular communication sent to current and potential investors showing progress, metrics, and specific needs
fundraising-fundamentals.example: A monthly email highlighting 20% user growth, a key new hire, and asking for introductions to potential enterprise customers

fundraising-fundamentals.definition: The maximum company valuation at which an investor's money converts to equity in a future priced round
fundraising-fundamentals.example: An investor provides $100,000 via SAFE with a $5M valuation cap, meaning their investment will convert as if the company were worth $5M even if the actual valuation is higher

mastering-tokenomics.definition: The rules and laws that influence behaviors within a blockchain ecosystem through incentives and disincentives
mastering-tokenomics.example: A protocol offering higher APY rewards to users who stake tokens for longer periods

mastering-tokenomics.definition: All stakeholders participating in a token ecosystem, including users, validators, liquidity providers, developers, and investors
mastering-tokenomics.example: In a DeFi protocol, liquidity providers contribute assets to pools and receive token rewards in return

mastering-tokenomics.definition: The practical use cases and reasons for holding or using a token beyond speculation
mastering-tokenomics.example: Offering a 30% discount when users pay for services using the platform's native token instead of fiat currency

mastering-tokenomics.definition: Methods used to acquire initial users and liquidity for a new Web3 project
mastering-tokenomics.example: A new DEX offering higher yield farming rewards during its first three months to attract liquidity providers

mastering-tokenomics.definition: The combined value of cryptocurrency assets deposited in a DeFi protocol
mastering-tokenomics.example: A lending protocol with $100M in TVL from user deposits across multiple asset types

pitching-fundamentals.definition: The organization of visual and verbal elements in a presentation designed to secure investment
pitching-fundamentals.example: Simple slides with visual cues paired with a memorized script that tells a complete story with beginning, middle, and end

pitching-fundamentals.definition: A storytelling structure that helps organize information in a way that resonates emotionally with listeners
pitching-fundamentals.example: The Bridge Story shows how your solution connects a significant problem with an innovative solution, creating a clear path forward

pitching-fundamentals.definition: A technique for explaining technical concepts by relating them to familiar ideas or experiences
pitching-fundamentals.example: Comparing a new token incentive mechanism to how loyalty programs encourage repeat customers at coffee shops

pitching-fundamentals.definition: A three-part structure for establishing credibility as an early-stage founder
pitching-fundamentals.example: Explaining market positioning (context), user journey (content), and both short-term actions and long-term projections (climb)

pitching-fundamentals.definition: A preparation technique that helps speakers connect emotionally to their material before presenting
pitching-fundamentals.example: Identifying the key topic 'community' for a section, connecting personally by reflecting on why building community matters to you, then choosing an appropriate energy level for delivery

access-restriction.term: What is a precompile in the Avalanche L1 context?
access-restriction.definition: A 'built-in contract' backed by the VM implementation (Go for Avalanche), mapped to a reserved address. From Solidity it looks like a normal contract callable via an interface, but it executes faster and can expose VM-level capabilities regular contracts can't safely implement.

access-restriction.term: Stateless precompile
access-restriction.definition: A precompile that performs pure computation with no persistent storage. It takes input, computes a result, and returns it without storing anything. Examples include cryptographic operations like ecrecover (signature recovery) and sha256.

access-restriction.term: Stateful precompile
access-restriction.definition: A precompile that can read and write data to the EVM state. For allowlist precompiles it stores role assignments (Admin, Manager, Enabled) per address. This data persists across blocks.

access-restriction.term: Transaction AllowList precompile purpose
access-restriction.definition: A stateful precompile that controls who can submit transactions to an Avalanche L1. If a wallet address is not allowed, transactions from that address will fail before execution. Genesis config key: txAllowListConfig.

access-restriction.term: Contract Deployer AllowList precompile purpose
access-restriction.definition: A stateful precompile that controls who can deploy smart contracts on an Avalanche L1. If an address is not allowed, contract deployment transactions will revert. Genesis config key: contractDeployerAllowListConfig.

access-restriction.term: Why use precompiles for access restriction instead of regular contracts?
access-restriction.definition: Access restriction is a network rule, not a typical dApp feature. Precompiles give performance and gas efficiency because checks happen at the VM level, security because the permission logic is centralized and audited rather than re-implemented in every contract, and consistency because multiple precompiles can share the same permission model via a common interface.

access-restriction.term: AllowList interface
access-restriction.definition: The shared, audited permission interface implemented by multiple default Avalanche L1 precompiles. It exposes a role-based access control pattern via setAdmin, setManager, setEnabled, setNone, and readAllowList functions.

access-restriction.term: Admin role in the AllowList interface
access-restriction.definition: The highest permission level in the AllowList interface. An Admin can manage all roles (Admin, Manager, Enabled) on the precompile.

access-restriction.term: Manager role in the AllowList interface
access-restriction.definition: A permission level in the AllowList interface that can manage Enabled addresses only. A Manager cannot grant or revoke Admin or Manager roles.

access-restriction.term: Enabled role in the AllowList interface
access-restriction.definition: A permission level that grants the right to use the precompile's functionality. What 'use' means depends on the specific precompile (for Transaction AllowList: submit transactions; for Contract Deployer AllowList: deploy contracts).

access-restriction.term: None role in the AllowList interface
access-restriction.definition: The default permission level meaning no access. An address with role None is not authorized to use the precompile's functionality.

access-restriction.term: setAdmin function on AllowList precompiles
access-restriction.definition: An AllowList interface function that gives the supplied address the Admin role on that precompile. Only callers with the Admin role may invoke it.

access-restriction.term: setManager function on AllowList precompiles
access-restriction.definition: An AllowList interface function that gives the supplied address the Manager role on that precompile. Only Admins may grant the Manager role.

access-restriction.term: setEnabled function on AllowList precompiles
access-restriction.definition: An AllowList interface function that gives the supplied address the Enabled role on that precompile. Both Admin and Manager callers may grant the Enabled role.

access-restriction.term: setNone function on AllowList precompiles
access-restriction.definition: An AllowList interface function that removes any role from the supplied address, returning it to the None state with no access.

access-restriction.term: readAllowList function signature
access-restriction.definition: An AllowList interface read function with signature readAllowList(address addr) -> uint256 that returns the current role value assigned to addr on the precompile.

access-restriction.term: Default precompiles that implement the AllowList interface
access-restriction.definition: Examples include Deployer AllowList, Transaction AllowList, Native Minter, Fee Manager, and Reward Manager. All five share the same role-based access control pattern.

access-restriction.term: KYC/AML gating use-case for access restriction
access-restriction.definition: An access control use-case in permissioned environments where only onboarded entities are allowed to transact or deploy contracts on the L1, satisfying Know Your Customer and Anti Money Laundering requirements.

access-restriction.term: Consortium network use-case for access restriction
access-restriction.definition: A set of known participants (banks, enterprises, or agencies) sharing a network where membership changes over time. Access restriction precompiles let the consortium control who participates.

access-restriction.term: Staged rollouts use-case for access restriction
access-restriction.definition: A pattern where an L1 starts with restricted access during pilot phases and then broadens access once monitoring and incident response are mature.

access-restriction.term: Reduce exploit surface use-case for access restriction
access-restriction.definition: Limiting deployers reduces the risk of malicious contract deployments, and limiting transactors mitigates spam and abuse on the L1.

access-restriction.term: Transaction AllowList precompile address
access-restriction.definition: The reserved address at which the Transaction AllowList precompile is mapped on an Avalanche L1: 0x0200000000000000000000000000000000000002.

access-restriction.term: Contract Deployer AllowList precompile address
access-restriction.definition: The reserved address at which the Contract Deployer AllowList precompile is mapped on an Avalanche L1: 0x0200000000000000000000000000000000000000.

access-restriction.term: Genesis block for activating allowlist precompiles
access-restriction.definition: To enable allowlist precompiles from genesis, include their config blocks (txAllowListConfig and contractDeployerAllowListConfig) in the genesis config with blockTimestamp set to 0 and an adminAddresses array.

access-restriction.term: adminAddresses field at genesis
access-restriction.definition: An array of EVM addresses included in a precompile's genesis config that become AllowList Admins for that precompile, able to manage Admin, Manager, and Enabled roles.

access-restriction.term: Role value returned by readAllowList for None
access-restriction.definition: The numeric role value 0, meaning the address has no access on that allowlist precompile.

access-restriction.term: Role value returned by readAllowList for Enabled
access-restriction.definition: The numeric role value 1, meaning the address is authorized to use the precompile's functionality but cannot manage other addresses.

access-restriction.term: Role value returned by readAllowList for Manager
access-restriction.definition: The numeric role value 2, meaning the address can grant the Enabled role to other addresses but cannot manage Admins or other Managers.

access-restriction.term: Role value returned by readAllowList for Admin
access-restriction.definition: The numeric role value 3, meaning the address can manage all roles (Admin, Manager, Enabled) on that precompile.

access-restriction.term: Why a Docker validator is required for network upgrades
access-restriction.definition: BuilderHub hosted nodes don't allow direct access to configuration files. To edit upgrade.json and restart the node to apply network upgrades, you need to run your own self-hosted AvalancheGo validator with full filesystem access.

access-restriction.term: When you call a precompile from Solidity, what code executes?
access-restriction.definition: You're not calling a deployed smart contract — you're invoking Go code built into the VM. The Solidity compiler emits a CALL opcode targeting the precompile's reserved address with ABI-encoded data, and the EVM routes the call to the registered precompile module.

access-restriction.term: PrecompileOverride hook
access-restriction.definition: A VM-level hook that intercepts CALL opcodes to reserved precompile addresses. It looks up whether the target address has a registered precompile module and routes execution to that module's Run function rather than normal contract execution.

access-restriction.term: Reserved address ranges for precompiles on Avalanche L1s
access-restriction.definition: Three reserved address ranges: 0x0100...00 to 0x0100...ff, 0x0200...00 to 0x0200...ff (where allowlist precompiles live), and 0x0300...00 to 0x0300...ff.

access-restriction.term: Function selector dispatch in a precompile
access-restriction.definition: Inside a precompile's Run function, the first 4 bytes of input data are parsed as a function selector and matched against known selectors (setAdmin, setEnabled, readAllowList, etc.) to determine which Go function executes.

access-restriction.term: Storage key used by allowlist precompiles
access-restriction.definition: Allowlist precompiles store an address's role in the EVM state trie under the key keccak256(address.Bytes()), with the value being a 32-byte hash containing the role byte.

access-restriction.term: Fixed gas cost to read a role from an allowlist precompile
access-restriction.definition: 5,000 gas. Allowlist precompiles have fixed gas costs rather than EVM-metered execution.

access-restriction.term: Fixed gas cost to write a role to an allowlist precompile
access-restriction.definition: 20,000 gas. Writing a role (setAdmin, setManager, setEnabled, setNone) is more expensive than reading because it modifies the state trie.

access-restriction.term: Three roles allowlist precompiles play in the VM
access-restriction.definition: Storage: persists address-to-role mappings always. Management: provides Solidity functions admins call to modify the database. Enforcement: rejects unauthorized transactions automatically, before every transaction, without the user calling the precompile.

access-restriction.term: Key insight about allowlist enforcement
access-restriction.definition: You can be blocked from sending ANY transaction without ever interacting with the precompile directly. The EVM checks the precompile's storage at hardcoded enforcement points in the VM code, not through Solidity calls.

access-restriction.term: Mempool validation checkpoint for Transaction AllowList
access-restriction.definition: The first enforcement checkpoint where the mempool reads keccak256(sender) from the TxAllowList storage at 0x0200...0002. If the role is NoRole, the transaction is rejected and never enters the mempool. This is fast, local, and prevents spam.

access-restriction.term: Pre-execution checkpoint for Transaction AllowList
access-restriction.definition: The second enforcement checkpoint during state transition. Validators re-read the sender's role from TxAllowList storage. If the role is NoRole, the transaction fails canonically with gas consumed and state reverted. This check is consensus-critical.

access-restriction.term: Why two enforcement checkpoints exist for Transaction AllowList
access-restriction.definition: Mempool check is fast, local, and prevents spam but is not consensus-binding. The execution check is canonical: validators must agree network-wide. State can change between checks — an admin could revoke permission after the tx entered the mempool but before it executes.

access-restriction.term: CanCreateContract hook
access-restriction.definition: An EVM hook executed before EVM.Create() during contract deployment. When the Contract Deployer AllowList precompile is enabled, the hook reads the role of tx.origin from DeployerAllowList storage and returns an error if the origin is not Enabled or Admin.

access-restriction.term: Which address the CanCreateContract hook checks
access-restriction.definition: tx.origin (the original transaction sender), not msg.sender. This means the original externally-owned account submitting the transaction must hold Enabled or Admin on the Contract Deployer AllowList, regardless of any contracts proxying the deployment.

access-restriction.term: Effect of disabling a precompile via upgrade.json
access-restriction.definition: Disabling prevents all calls to the precompile and destructs its storage. This allows the precompile to be re-enabled later with fresh configuration (new adminAddresses) since previous role assignments are wiped.

access-restriction.term: Location of upgrade.json on a node
access-restriction.definition: It must live next to the chain's config.json at the path {chain-config-dir}/{blockchainID}/upgrade.json. On a typical node that is ~/.avalanchego/configs/chains/<BLOCKCHAIN_ID>/upgrade.json.

access-restriction.term: Append-only rule for precompileUpgrades
access-restriction.definition: Once an upgrade activates (a block after its blockTimestamp is accepted), that entry must remain in upgrade.json exactly as it was. You can only append new entries, never modify or remove activated entries — otherwise the node may refuse to start.

access-restriction.term: Future timestamp rule for upgrade.json
access-restriction.definition: Every blockTimestamp in precompileUpgrades must be in the future relative to the chain head when the node starts. A past timestamp on a not-yet-activated entry can cause the node to fail on startup, so set timestamps to (now + a few minutes) when authoring upgrades.

access-restriction.term: Disable flag syntax in upgrade.json
access-restriction.definition: To disable a precompile via network upgrade, append an entry with the precompile's config key, a future blockTimestamp, and "disable": true.

access-restriction.term: Re-enabling an allowlist precompile after a disable upgrade
access-restriction.definition: Because disabling clears precompile storage, re-enabling is a fresh initialization. You must append a new precompileUpgrades entry with a future blockTimestamp greater than any prior timestamp and an adminAddresses array so you can manage roles after activation.

avalanche-fundamentals.definition: A leaderless, decentralized, and scalable consensus protocol used in the Avalanche network to agree on the state of the network through repeated sub-sampled voting among validators.

avalanche-fundamentals.term: Purpose of a consensus mechanism in a blockchain
avalanche-fundamentals.definition: To resolve conflicts and ensure all validators agree on the current state of the distributed ledger by creating a single version of truth universally accepted by network participants.

An attack where a user attempts to spend more crypto than they own by creating multiple transactions that reference the same funds. Validators must reach consensus on which of the conflicting transactions is included first.
A validator asks a small, random subset of other validators about their current preference, and based on the responses it may change its own preference until a decision threshold is reached.
Sample Size: how many participants get queried each round of sub-sampling. Range is 1 to n, where n is the total number of participants in the system.
Quorum Size: how many of the k sampled participants must share the same preference for a validator to change its own preference to that option. Range is 1 to k.
Decision Threshold: how many consecutive rounds the same preference must be confirmed before a validator finalizes its decision. Range is >= 1.
Guarantees with high probability (based on system parameters) that if any honest validator accepts a transaction, all honest validators will come to the same conclusion.
Transactions Per Second: a measure of how many transactions are finalized per second on a blockchain. Higher TPS means more transactions can be processed in a given time frame.
The duration from when a transaction is submitted to validators until it becomes unchangeable. Avalanche achieves a TTF of approximately 0.8 seconds.
Avalanche / Avalanche L1 reaches roughly 2500 TPS with a time to finality of about 0.8 seconds. Bitcoin: 7 TPS / 60 min. Ethereum: 30 TPS / 6.4 min.

The foundational layer of the Avalanche ecosystem, consisting of three built-in blockchains (P-Chain, C-Chain, X-Chain) that all validators must validate. It coordinates and secures the entire Avalanche network.

Coordinates validators, tracks active L1s, and manages staking. It runs the special-purpose virtual machine called platformvm, is not EVM-based, and stores node ID, BLS Public Key, Stake Weight, and Balance for each validator.
An EVM-compatible blockchain in the Avalanche Primary Network used for smart contracts and DeFi applications.
The Avalanche Primary Network chain optimized for creating and transferring digital assets.

The native token of the Avalanche ecosystem used to pay transaction fees on the C-Chain, stake to become a validator, secure the network through Proof-of-Stake consensus, and serve as a common unit of account.
An independent blockchain with its own set of rules regarding validator membership, tokenomics, and execution layer. It has its own validator set that comes to consensus and validates the blockchain.
L1s do not share execution threads, storage, or networking with other L1s or the Primary Network. They specify their own execution logic, fee structures, state, networking, and security.

avalanche-fundamentals.term: Avalanche L1 vs Layer 2 security model
avalanche-fundamentals.definition: Each Avalanche L1 maintains its own security as part of the base layer, so a compromise in one L1 does not impact others. Layer 2 rollups delegate security to the Ethereum mainnet, so a mainnet breach can affect all L2s.

A blockchain validated by a subset of Primary Network validators. Each Primary Network validator could be a member of multiple Subnets but was required to also meet the 2,000 AVAX Primary Network staking requirement.

avalanche-fundamentals.term: Steps to create an L1 on Avalanche
avalanche-fundamentals.definition: 1) Issue CreateSubnetTx on the P-Chain to create a Subnet record. 2) Issue CreateChainTx to add one or more chains. 3) Issue ConvertSubnetToL1Tx to convert the Subnet to a sovereign L1 and set initial validators.
A P-Chain transaction with a single parameter (the Subnet owner) that creates a Subnet record uniquely identified by the transaction hash. The owner can add blockchains and convert it to an L1.
name (chain name), subnetID (Subnet to attach to), vmID (Virtual Machine ID for the chain), and genesisData (genesis configuration of the chain).

A one-time P-Chain transaction by the Subnet owner that establishes the new validator set for the blockchain and transforms it from a Subnet into a sovereign L1. After conversion the owner loses all privileges.
Subnet ID, Validator Manager Blockchain ID (blockchain hosting the contract), Validator Manager Address (contract address), and the initial set of Validators.

avalanche-fundamentals.term: Validator manager contract role after L1 conversion
avalanche-fundamentals.definition: After the ConvertSubnetToL1Tx, the L1 is controlled by a validator manager contract that manages the validator set; the Subnet owner loses all privileges.

Initially the Validator Manager Address points to an OpenZeppelin TransparentUpgradeableProxy contract pre-deployed in genesis.json. After conversion, the actual ValidatorManager implementation is deployed and the proxy is updated to point to it.

avalanche-fundamentals.term: Per-validator information stored on the P-Chain
avalanche-fundamentals.definition: A unique node ID identifying the validator, a BLS Public Key, the Stake Weight, and the Balance of the validator for the continuous interoperability fee.
The special-purpose virtual machine that the P-Chain runs. It is not EVM-based, so interacting with it requires a compatible wallet like Core wallet.
L1 validators sync the P-Chain to always have the latest view of the validator set across all Avalanche blockchains, but they do not participate in the consensus of the P-Chain itself.
Core is the only wallet that supports issuing P-Chain transactions. MetaMask and Rabby cannot be used to interact with the P-Chain.
The client software run by validator nodes that secures Avalanche blockchains. Each L1 in the Avalanche network has its own set of validators running the AvalancheGo client.
Use Docker to stop and remove a node. The state and credentials persist in ~/.avalanchego unless that directory is deleted.
avalanche-fundamentals.example:
docker stop avago
docker rm avago
rm -rf ~/.avalanchego

avalanche-fundamentals.definition: Free managed testnet nodes provided through Builder Console automatically shut down after 3 days. For production or extended testing, self-hosted Docker infrastructure should be used.
With a Builder Account, testnet AVAX can be claimed on the C-Chain and P-Chain directly from the Builder Console without a coupon code or holding a mainnet balance.
Without a Builder Hub Account, users can use the external Avalanche Faucet with coupon code `avalanche-academy` or `avalanche-academy25` to get 2 AVAX on the C-Chain.

The ability of different blockchain networks to communicate, share data, and interact with each other without intermediaries, allowing assets, information, and functionalities to move between separate ecosystems.
The original blockchain in cross-blockchain communication where data, assets, or information originate before being communicated. It is the starting point for initiating cross-chain interactions.
The recipient blockchain in cross-chain communication that receives, authenticates, and processes the data, assets, or instructions relayed from the source chain.

Contains source, destination, and encoded data along with a signature. The signature attests the authenticity of the message, guaranteeing that whatever the message claims actually happened on the source chain.

The foundation of cross-chain communication on Avalanche, built directly into every Avalanche blockchain as a native feature. It lets contracts create messages, validators sign them, and destination chains verify them.
A special smart contract pre-installed on every Avalanche blockchain that is built into the blockchain software itself (written in Go, unlike most contracts in Solidity). It is the heart of ICM.

An ICM contract that provides a developer-friendly interface for cross-chain messaging. It exposes a `sendCrossChainMessage()` function, is deployed at the same address on every blockchain, and handles encoding, tracking, fees, and replay protection.

A permissionless service that monitors blockchains for new cross-chain messages, collects validator signatures, submits the transaction on the destination chain, and pays gas fees (reimbursed via Teleporter fees).

An application built on top of ICM, Teleporter, and relayers that enables asset transfers between L1s by locking tokens on the source chain (Token Home) and minting wrapped representations on destination chains (Token Remote).

avalanche-fundamentals.term: Token Home vs Token Remote in ICTT
avalanche-fundamentals.definition: Token Home manages the original tokens on their native blockchain. Token Remote creates wrapped versions of those tokens on other blockchains. ICTT handles locking, minting, burning, and releasing of tokens.
Authenticity (proves the message came from the claimed sender), Non-Repudiation (the signer cannot deny involvement), and Integrity (the message has not been tampered with during transmission).

GenerateKeyPair() returns (PrivateKey, PublicKey); Sign(Message, PrivateKey) returns Signature; Verify(Message, Signature, PublicKey) returns Valid/Invalid. Some schemes also expose Recover(Message, Signature) returning the PublicKey.

The Boneh-Lynn-Shacham multi-signature scheme is notable for its compact size. It supports signature aggregation (compresses many signatures into one short signature) and public key aggregation (verification uses one short aggregated public key).

Without aggregation, 1000 BLS signatures of 96 bytes each would total 96,000 bytes. Aggregating into a single compact signature makes it efficient to send a message signed by a large portion of Primary Network validators to a destination chain for verification.

customizing-evm.definition: A quasi-Turing complete machine that executes smart contracts in a sandboxed environment isolated from the main network. Proposed by Vitalik Buterin in late 2013 and launched on July 30, 2015. Smart contracts written in Solidity or Vyper are compiled to EVM bytecode for execution.
customizing-evm.example: The EVM runs smart contracts deployed on Ethereum and EVM-compatible chains like the C-Chain.

customizing-evm.definition: An EVM account controlled by a private key with no associated code. EOAs can send transactions by signing them with their private key and are typically used by end users.
customizing-evm.example: A user wallet like Core or MetaMask controls an EOA via its private key.

customizing-evm.definition: An EVM account that has associated code (smart contract bytecode). Contract accounts cannot initiate transactions on their own and only execute when called by an EOA.
customizing-evm.example: A deployed ERC-20 token contract is a Contract Account that runs only when invoked.

customizing-evm.definition: A 20-byte EVM address is derived by passing the 64-byte ECDSA public key through Keccak-256, then taking the rightmost 20 bytes (40 hex chars) and prefixing with 0x for a total of 42 characters.
customizing-evm.example: Public key 64 bytes -> Keccak-256 -> rightmost 20 bytes -> 0xe66eb0ce272fc0f4358c57a8f0a442410c3d831a

customizing-evm.definition: The Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm generates a 32-byte private key and a 64-byte public key (two concatenated 32-byte coordinates) used to sign transactions on Ethereum and EVM chains.
customizing-evm.example: Private key (32 bytes, random) -> Public key (64 bytes, two 32-byte coordinates)

customizing-evm.definition: A unique sender-set value that ensures each transaction is processed exactly once, preventing replay attacks on EVM chains.
customizing-evm.example: If a transaction with nonce 5 is mined, the same nonce cannot be reused.

customizing-evm.term: V, R, S in an EVM transaction
customizing-evm.definition: Cryptographic values that together form the sender's ECDSA signature over a transaction, used to recover the signer's address and verify authenticity.
customizing-evm.example: The V, R, S fields let validators verify the EOA that authorized a transaction.

customizing-evm.definition: A hash of the entire Ethereum state after executing all transactions in the block. Together with Transactions Root and Receipts Root it commits to the post-block world state.
customizing-evm.example: The State Root in block header changes whenever a transaction modifies any account balance or storage.

customizing-evm.definition: The official Ethereum client (go-ethereum) written in Go. It handles transactions, deploys and executes smart contracts, and contains the EVM. It is one of the original and most widely used Ethereum clients.
customizing-evm.example: Repository: https://github.com/ethereum/go-ethereum

customizing-evm.definition: A fork of Geth maintained by Ava Labs that implements the EVM for the C-Chain and is adapted to work with Avalanche Consensus.
customizing-evm.example: Coreth lives at github.com/ava-labs/avalanchego/tree/master/graft/coreth

customizing-evm.definition: A fork of Coreth designed to launch customized EVM-based blockchains on an Avalanche L1. Differs from Coreth by supporting configurable fees/gas limits, merging all Avalanche hardforks into a single Subnet-EVM hardfork, and removing atomic transactions, shared memory, and multicoin contracts/state.
customizing-evm.example: Repository: https://github.com/ava-labs/subnet-evm

customizing-evm.definition: A wrapper that allows precompiles to be registered with Subnet-EVM without needing to fork the Subnet-EVM codebase, simplifying common customizations and streamlining Subnet-EVM updates.
customizing-evm.example: Importing a precompile package in plugin/main.go triggers self-registration with Subnet-EVM.

customizing-evm.definition: A unique identifier introduced in EIP-155 that distinguishes EVM chains from one another and prevents cross-chain transaction replay attacks. Set in the top-level chainId field of the genesis JSON.
customizing-evm.example: "chainId": 99999 in genesis JSON; verify uniqueness on chainlist.org including testnets.

customizing-evm.definition: A special Avalanche L1 consisting of the Contract (C), Platform (P), and Exchange (X) chains. It is not a separate standalone network but a privileged L1 every Avalanche node validates.
customizing-evm.example: C-Chain, P-Chain, and X-Chain together form the Primary Network.

customizing-evm.definition: A command-line tool that gives developers access to Avalanche functionality for building and testing independent blockchains. Supports starting/stopping local networks, health checks, and creating blockchains with custom parameters.
customizing-evm.example: avalanche blockchain create myblockchain --custom --vm $VM_PATH

customizing-evm.definition: Foundry command used to send a signed transaction to an RPC endpoint. Requires --rpc-url, a signing key (e.g., --private-key), the recipient address, and optional value or calldata.
customizing-evm.example: cast send --rpc-url myblockchain --private-key $PK 0x321f6B73... --value 1ether

customizing-evm.definition: The initial block of an EVM L1 whose JSON file contains chainId, feeConfig, alloc (initial balances), precompile config keys, and standard EVM fields. The same VM with different genesis blocks yields different blockchains.
customizing-evm.example: Genesis fields: chainId, feeConfig, allowFeeRecipients, alloc, gasLimit, parentHash, mixHash, coinbase.

customizing-evm.definition: Sets the maximum gas consumed per block, capping the computational capacity of a single block and the maximum gas usage of any single transaction. The standard EVM top-level gasLimit field must match this value.
customizing-evm.example: C-Chain feeConfig.gasLimit is set to 15000000.

customizing-evm.definition: The target block production rate in seconds in Subnet-EVM feeConfig. If blocks are produced faster than this rate, base fees increase; if slower, base fees decrease.
customizing-evm.example: C-Chain targetBlockRate is 1; the customizing-EVM example uses 2.

customizing-evm.definition: A lower bound on the EIP-1559 base fee per unit of gas for a block, effectively setting the minimum gas price for any transaction included in that block.
customizing-evm.example: "minBaseFee": 25000000000 means 25 gwei minimum gas price.

customizing-evm.definition: The targeted amount of gas (including block gas cost) to be consumed within a rolling 10-second window. The dynamic fee algorithm scales the base fee proportionally to how actual usage compares to this target.
customizing-evm.example: "targetGas": 15000000 with targetBlockRate 2 implies ~30M gas / 10s window.

customizing-evm.definition: Controls how quickly the base fee adjusts to the difference between actual and target gas utilization. A larger denominator yields slower changes; a smaller one yields faster changes.
customizing-evm.example: C-Chain uses 36, meaning the base fee changes by 1/36 of the parent block's base fee per step.

customizing-evm.definition: Defines how much to increase or decrease the block gas cost for each second faster or slower than the targetBlockRate. If a block is produced at target rate, the block gas cost is unchanged.
customizing-evm.example: "blockGasCostStep": 200000 adjusts block gas cost by 200000 per second of deviation.

customizing-evm.definition: A genesis chainConfig field on Subnet-EVM that determines whether gas fees are paid to a fee recipient (validator) or follow the default behavior. Allows the L1 designer to decide if fees are burned, paid to validators, or used for other custom behavior.
customizing-evm.example: "allowFeeRecipients": false in the customizing-EVM example.

customizing-evm.definition: Defines initial balances of addresses at chain creation. Keys are hex addresses without the 0x prefix; balance is decimal or hex Wei (10^18 Wei = 1 native token). Without alloc, no funds exist to pay gas.
The default test wallet address 0x8db97C7cEcE249c2b98bDC0226Cc4C2A57BF52FC used by Avalanche Academy on local test networks. Its private key is publicly known and must never be used for real funds.
customizing-evm.example: Private key: 56289e99c94b6912bfc12adc093c9b51124f0dc54ac7a766b2bc5ccf558d8027

customizing-evm.definition: Code implemented in Go at a reserved EVM address that executes faster and more efficiently than Solidity. When a Solidity contract calls one of these reserved addresses, the EVM runs the Go precompile instead of bytecode.

The Go interface that all Avalanche L1-EVM precompiles must implement. Run receives AccessibleState, caller, addr, input bytes, suppliedGas, and readOnly; returns ret bytes, remainingGas, and an error.
customizing-evm.example: Run(accessibleState AccessibleState, caller common.Address, addr common.Address, input []byte, suppliedGas uint64, readOnly bool) (ret []byte, remainingGas uint64, err error)

customizing-evm.definition: The 0x01... address range is reserved for precompiles added by Ethereum (e.g., ecrecover, SHA256, RIPEMD160, identity).
The 0x02... address range is reserved for precompiles provided by Avalanche.
customizing-evm.example: Custom Avalanche precompiles for native protocol features sit in the 0x02 range.

customizing-evm.definition: The 0x03... address range is reserved for custom precompiles built with Precompile-EVM/Subnet-EVM by L1 developers.
customizing-evm.example: var ContractAddress = common.HexToAddress("0x0300000000000000000000000000000000000002")

customizing-evm.definition: A unique string defined in a precompile's module.go that is used in the chainConfig of genesis JSON to activate the precompile at a given blockTimestamp. Must be unique across all precompiles.
customizing-evm.example: const ConfigKey = "md5Config"  // and in genesis: "md5Config": { "blockTimestamp": 0 }

customizing-evm.definition: Each generated precompile package has a self-registering init function. Importing the package with a blank identifier in plugin/main.go is what registers it with Subnet-EVM.
customizing-evm.example: _ "github.com/ava-labs/precompile-evm/md5"

customizing-evm.definition: Script provided by Precompile-EVM that generates Go boilerplate for a precompile from an ABI file. Takes --abi, --type, --pkg, and --out flags and produces config.go, contract.go, module.go, and tests.
customizing-evm.example: ./scripts/generate_precompile.sh --abi ./contracts/abis/IMD5.abi --type Md5 --pkg md5 --out ./md5

customizing-evm.definition: A generated Go function that decodes the raw input bytes (without the 4-byte function selector) into typed Go values matching the Solidity interface's arguments.
customizing-evm.example: func UnpackHashWithMD5Input(input []byte) (string, error) { ... Md5ABI.UnpackInput("hashWithMD5", input) ... }

customizing-evm.definition: A generated Go function that ABI-encodes the precompile's return values into a byte slice matching the Solidity function's return type, suitable for being returned from Run.
customizing-evm.example: func PackHashWithMD5Output(hash [16]byte) ([]byte, error) { return Md5ABI.PackOutput("hashWithMD5", hash) }

customizing-evm.definition: Helper function used at the start of every precompile function to subtract the function's gas cost from suppliedGas. Returns remainingGas or vmerrs.ErrOutOfGas if insufficient gas was supplied.
customizing-evm.example: if remainingGas, err = contract.DeductGas(suppliedGas, HashWithMD5GasCost); err != nil { return nil, 0, err }

customizing-evm.definition: When a Solidity function in the precompile interface has more than one input or output, the generator creates Go structs (e.g., AddInput, NextTwoOutput) to bundle the values; single-value functions use plain types like *big.Int.
customizing-evm.example: type ModuloPlusInput struct { Dividend *big.Int; Divisor *big.Int }

customizing-evm.definition: Inside hashWithMD5, the unpacked string is hashed via md5.Sum from crypto/md5 to produce a [16]byte; the result is then packed via PackHashWithMD5Output and returned with the remaining gas.
customizing-evm.example: output = md5.Sum([]byte(inputStruct)); packedOutput, _ := PackHashWithMD5Output(output)

customizing-evm.definition: moduloPlus uses big.Int.DivMod to set both Multiple (quotient) and Remainder in a single call against the unpacked Dividend and Divisor.
customizing-evm.example: output.Multiple, output.Remainder = big.NewInt(0).DivMod(inputStruct.Dividend, inputStruct.Divisor, output.Remainder)

customizing-evm.definition: When the denominator is 0 in simplFrac, both SimplNum and SimplDenom are returned as 0. Otherwise the GCD is computed via big.Int.GCD and numerator and denominator are each divided by the GCD.
customizing-evm.example: if inputStruct.Denominator.Cmp(big.NewInt(0)) == 0 { output.SimplDenom = big.NewInt(0); output.SimplNum = big.NewInt(0) } else { var gcd big.Int; gcd.GCD(nil, nil, inputStruct.Numerator, inputStruct.Denominator); ... }

customizing-evm.definition: The Go interface passed to stateful precompiles exposing GetStateDB(), GetBlockContext(), and GetSnowContext(). It is how precompiles read and modify EVM state, query block metadata, and access network/chain identifiers.
customizing-evm.example: type AccessibleState interface { GetStateDB() StateDB; GetBlockContext() BlockContext; GetSnowContext() *snow.Context }

customizing-evm.definition: EVM persistent storage exposed to precompiles as a mapping where the key is a tuple of (address, 32-byte storage key Hash) and the value is a 32-byte Hash (a word). StateDB.GetState and SetState are used to read and write these slots.
customizing-evm.example: stateDB.SetState(ContractAddress, storageKeyHash, newValueHash)

customizing-evm.definition: Go-ethereum helper that converts a byte slice into a 32-byte Hash by interpreting it as a hex value; it does NOT cryptographically hash the input, it just produces a fixed-length 32-byte representation.
customizing-evm.example: storageKeyHash = common.BytesToHash([]byte("counterValue"))

customizing-evm.definition: Go-ethereum helper that left-pads a byte slice with zeros up to a target length (typically common.HashLength = 32) so values like strings or numbers can be stored as a single EVM word.
customizing-evm.example: inputPadded := common.LeftPadBytes(value.Bytes(), 32)  // pad to 32 bytes

customizing-evm.definition: The EVM error returned by stateful precompile write functions (e.g., setString, setCounter, incrementCounter) when called with readOnly=true, preventing state mutation inside a static call context.
customizing-evm.example: if readOnly { return nil, remainingGas, vmerrs.ErrWriteProtection }

customizing-evm.definition: A helper that converts a *big.Int counter to a left-padded 32-byte Hash and writes it to the precompile's storage slot via stateDB.SetState(ContractAddress, storageKeyHash, inputHash).
customizing-evm.example: func StoreCounterValue(stateDB contract.StateDB, value *big.Int) { inputPadded := common.LeftPadBytes(value.Bytes(), 32); inputHash := common.BytesToHash(inputPadded); stateDB.SetState(ContractAddress, storageKeyHash, inputHash) }

customizing-evm.definition: The precompile module's Configure function is called once by the EVM per precompile activation. It receives the parsed Config plus a StateDB and is the place to seed initial state from genesis fields.
customizing-evm.example: func (*configurator) Configure(chainConfig precompileconfig.ChainConfig, cfg precompileconfig.Config, state contract.StateDB, blockContext contract.ConfigurationBlockContext) error { config, ok := cfg.(*Config); ...; StoreString(state, config.DefaultString); return nil }

customizing-evm.definition: To pass defaults via genesis JSON, add a tagged field to the precompile's Config struct (which embeds precompileconfig.Upgrade) and read it inside Configure. The struct tag binds the JSON key to the Go field.
customizing-evm.example: type Config struct { precompileconfig.Upgrade; DefaultString string `json:"defaultString,omitempty"` }

customizing-evm.definition: Environment variable used with ./scripts/run_ginkgo.sh in Precompile-EVM to select which test suite from suites.go to execute. Set to a specific precompile label or to "Precompile" to run all.
customizing-evm.example: GINKGO_LABEL_FILTER=StringStore ./scripts/run_ginkgo.sh

erc20-bridge.definition: A bridging mechanism where the asset is locked in a smart contract on the source blockchain and an equivalent amount of a wrapped token is minted on the target blockchain to the user's address.
erc20-bridge.example: Lock AVAX on Avalanche C-Chain, mint Wrapped AVAX on Ethereum.

erc20-bridge.definition: A bridging mechanism where wrapped tokens are burned or destroyed on the target blockchain and the original assets are released from the smart contract on the source blockchain back to the user's address.
A bridge model that uses a centralized party to manage assets, locking the asset on one blockchain and releasing it on another, relying on trust and centralized security measures.

An advanced bridge type such as Avalanche Interchain Token Transfer that uses native cross-chain communication protocols to facilitate transactions between blockchains without intermediaries, synchronizing asset state and ownership across chains.

A smart contract exploit that abuses a contract's ability to call itself, allowing attackers to repeatedly withdraw funds before the contract's state is updated. Used in the 2016 DAO hack to drain millions of ETH.
A smart contract vulnerability caused by integer overflows or underflows that leads to unintended behavior. The 2020 Value DeFi exploit used an arithmetic bug to drain $6 million from the protocol.
Errors in a contract's logic that can be exploited to bypass security controls. The bZx exploit used a logic flaw to manipulate asset prices and steal funds.

A 2021 centralized bridge attack involving a vulnerability that let attackers exploit the bridge's central control mechanisms and steal over $600 million, although much was later returned. Highlighted risks in centralized bridges.
A 2022 cross-chain communication attack exploiting a vulnerability in the bridge's data verification process, leading to a loss of over $320 million.
A governance attack on decentralized bridges where attackers manipulate the governance voting process to make changes that benefit themselves, gaining control or access to bridge assets.
An attack where an adversary gains control over a majority of governance participants or network nodes to disrupt or exploit a decentralized bridge with weak governance structures.
An attack that targets users to steal their private keys or credentials by tricking them into entering them on fraudulent websites pretending to be legitimate bridge interfaces.
A mitigation strategy requiring regular audits and code reviews of smart contracts and bridge code to identify and fix vulnerabilities before they can be exploited by attackers.
A demo bridge at ohmywarp.com used to mint a TLP ERC20 token on Fuji C-Chain and bridge it to the Dispatch L1. Requires a web3 wallet connected to the Fuji Testnet and some AVAX for gas.
An ERC20 token deployed on Fuji's C-Chain that learners mint in the ohmywarp Mint tab and bridge to Dispatch to confirm a cross-chain transfer.

An application that allows users to transfer tokens between Avalanche L1s using a set of smart contracts deployed across multiple L1s, built on top of Interchain Messaging (ICM) for cross-chain communication.

An ICTT deployment consisting of one home contract and at least one but possibly many remote contracts. The home contract manages one asset to be transferred out to TokenRemote instances on other Avalanche L1s.
ICTT property where anyone can register compatible TokenRemote instances to transfer tokens from a TokenHome instance to that new TokenRemote, without requiring approval from the home contract owner.

ICTT supports four home-to-remote combinations: ERC20 to ERC20, ERC20 to Native, Native to ERC20, and Native to Native, giving users any combination of ERC20 and native tokens between home and remote chains.

An interface in the ICTT contract structure that defines the events token transfer contract implementations must emit and the message types and formats of messages exchanged between all token transferrer implementations.

An ICTT interface defining external functions for ERC20 token transferrer implementations. Functions are not payable and require an explicit amount parameter, unlike the native token transferrer counterpart.
An ICTT interface defining external functions for native token transferrer implementations. Functions are payable and do not take an explicit amount parameter because it is implied by msg.value.

An abstract implementation of ITokenTransferrer on the home chain that holds the asset. Each TokenHome instance supports exactly one token type and can serve arbitrarily many TokenRemote instances on other chains by locking tokens or releasing collateral.

In a multi-hop transfer, the TokenHome already has the collateral locked from the initial transfer to the first TokenRemote, so it simply updates the accounting of transferred balances between the source and destination TokenRemote instances.

Remote contracts must first be registered with a TokenHome instance before the home contract will allow sending tokens to them. This prevents tokens from being transferred to invalid remote addresses.
A concrete implementation of TokenHome and IERC20TokenTransferrer that handles the locking and releasing of an ERC20 token on the home chain.
A concrete implementation of TokenHome and INativeTokenTransferrer that handles locking and releasing of the native EVM asset on the home chain.

An abstract implementation of ITokenTransferrer on a remote chain that receives transferred assets from a specific TokenHome instance. Each TokenRemote has a single specified TokenHome from which it mints tokens, and burns tokens to route back to other chains.

A function called on a deployed TokenRemote instance to register it with its specified TokenHome contract. It sends an Interchain Messaging message to the home contract with the information needed to register the remote.

Routing tokens from one TokenRemote instance to another by first sending them back to the TokenHome contract to update accounting, then automatically routing them on to the intended destination TokenRemote instance.

TokenRemote contracts allow scaling token amounts, used when the remote asset has a higher or lower denomination than the home asset, such as a denomination-6 ERC20 home asset used as the native EVM asset (denomination 18) on a remote chain.

A concrete implementation of TokenRemote, IERC20TokenTransferrer, and IERC20 that handles minting and burning of an ERC20 asset. The contract is itself an ERC20 implementation, so its constructor takes tokenName, tokenSymbol, and tokenDecimals.

A concrete implementation of TokenRemote, INativeTokenTransferrer, and IWrappedNativeToken that handles minting and burning of the native EVM asset using the native minter precompile. Always has a denomination of 18 and requires nativeAssetSymbol in its constructor.

Deployments of NativeTokenRemote must be permitted to mint native coins in the chain's configuration, since NativeTokenRemote uses the native minter precompile to mint the native EVM asset on the remote chain.

erc20-bridge.term: ERC20 to ERC20 bridge tutorial chains
erc20-bridge.definition: The ERC-20 to ERC-20 bridge chapter sends an ERC-20 from Fuji C-Chain to the Echo L1 testnet using Interchain Messaging and the Builders Hub Toolbox.
The official GitHub repository at ava-labs/avalanche-interchain-token-transfer that maintains all ICTT contracts and interfaces used in the ERC-20 to ERC-20 bridge chapter.

erc20-bridge.term: Steps to bridge an ERC-20 with ICTT
erc20-bridge.definition: The four-step ICTT deployment flow: (1) Deploy an ERC-20 contract on C-Chain, (2) deploy the Interchain Token Transferer contracts on C-Chain and Echo, (3) register the remote token contract with the home transferer, (4) add collateral and send tokens.

Two ways to get testnet AVAX: create a Builder Hub account at build.avax.network/login and connect a wallet for automatic distribution, or use the external Core faucet with coupon code avalanche-academy.

The Builders Hub toolbox component used to deploy the ERC20Home contract on Fuji C-Chain (chainId 43113). Requires setting the transferrer type to ERC20 and providing the previously deployed ERC-20 token address.
The Echo testnet L1 used as the destination chain in the ERC-20 to ERC-20 bridge tutorial has chain ID 173750.

The interface a remote contract must implement when bridging a wrapped ERC-20 into the destination chain. The remote must also inherit TeleporterTokenRemote and a standard ERC20 to have all normal local ERC20 functionality.

A one-time setup after deploying both home and remote bridge contracts where the RegisterWithHome action informs the Home Bridge about the destination blockchain and bridge settings, establishing the connection.

The Builders Hub toolbox component used to initiate a cross-chain transfer of an ERC-20 token from Fuji C-Chain (chainId 43113) to the destination L1 after deploying and registering the bridge contracts.
ICTT bridges deployed through AvaCloud automatically integrate into the Core Bridge at core.app/bridge, so they are available immediately and do not require extra manual review by the Core team.

ICTT bridges deployed outside of AvaCloud must be submitted for manual review. Developers provide the token bridge contract address(es) on the L1 and Layer 1 information such as the chain name and key details for the Core team to verify security and compliance.

An ICTT integration component that handles gas costs for transactions made through the bridge on users' behalf, forwarding user-signed transactions to the destination chain so users do not need to hold or manage gas tokens.

A relayer setup includes three components: a relaying node to handle transactions, a relayer wallet funded with gas tokens from the L1, and a trusted forwarder contract that securely relays user-signed transactions.
The Echo L1 chain is defined in BuilderKit using viem's defineChain with id 173750, network 'echo', native currency symbol ECH (18 decimals), and RPC https://subnets.avax.network/echo/testnet/rpc.
erc20-bridge.example:
import { defineChain } from 'viem';
export const echo = defineChain({ id: 173750, name: 'Echo L1', network: 'echo', nativeCurrency: { decimals: 18, name: 'Ech', symbol: 'ECH' }, rpcUrls: { default: { http: ['https://subnets.avax.network/echo/testnet/rpc'] } } });

erc20-bridge.definition: A BuilderKit token configuration flag indicating that the token on the current chain is a transferer — a contract responsible for moving the token between chains via Interchain Token Transfers.
The multi-hop chapter uses Echo as the source remote Ra, Dispatch as the destination remote Rb, and the Avalanche C-Chain as the home H. Tokens flow Ra to H to Rb in one logical multi-hop transfer.

interchain-messaging.definition: The ability of different blockchain networks to communicate and interact with one another, sharing data, assets, or functionalities so they work together as if part of a single unified network. Common forms include asset bridging (tokens, NFTs), DAO voting across chains, and cross-chain liquidity pools.

The original blockchain in cross-chain communication where data, assets, or information originate before being communicated to another blockchain. The sender calls a contract on the source chain to initiate the cross-chain interaction.

The recipient blockchain in cross-chain communication that receives and processes the communicated data, assets, or instructions. The destination submits the message as a transaction and verifies signatures before executing the specified instructions.

A data structure sent to the destination chain containing three parts: metadata identifying source and destination, the encoded message payload, and a signature attesting authenticity (that what the message claims actually happened on the source chain).

Multi-chain systems scale by running independent chains in parallel, producing aggregate throughput that exceeds any single chain. Unlike roll-ups which maximize one chain's throughput, multi-chain systems have no theoretical limit on the number of parallel chains.

Finality ensures that transactions on the source chain are confirmed and immutable, so any action triggered on the destination chain can be securely executed knowing the source chain state induced by the cross-chain message will not revert.

A correct Cross-Chain Communication protocol must have Effectiveness (all relevant transactions posted at the desired time), Atomicity (all-or-nothing posting), and Timeliness (transactions eventually posted on respective chains).

Per the paper SoK: Communication Across Distributed Ledgers, a trustless and correct cross-chain communication protocol is impossible. Using computational complexity theory, the problem reduces to the Fair Exchange problem, which has been proven to have no solution.

Also known as Teleporter, ICM is a protocol built on top of Avalanche Warp Messaging (AWM) that makes cross-chain communication developer-friendly. While AWM provides low-level signing and verification primitives, ICM adds message formatting, delivery tracking, receipts, and fee management.

ICM has three layers: AWM at the bottom (BLS signatures, Warp precompile, P-Chain validator signing/verification); ICM/Teleporter in the middle (TeleporterMessenger, TeleporterRegistry, formatting, delivery, fees); and dApps at the top (ERC-20 Bridge, Native Token Bridge, custom applications).

The core ICM contract deployed on every chain that participates in cross-chain communication. It provides a single entry point via sendCrossChainMessage(), handles delivery on the receiving side, assigns unique message IDs, tracks delivery, manages receipts, and coordinates fees.

TeleporterMessenger uses a deterministic deployment mechanism, meaning it is deployed to the same address on every chain. This is achieved by using a known deployer address with a specific nonce and requires funding the deployer with approximately 11 native tokens for gas.

The single-function Solidity interface a contract must implement to receive cross-chain messages from TeleporterMessenger. The TeleporterMessenger calls receiveTeleporterMessage when delivering a message; the receiver handles its own decoding logic.

A built-in Solidity function that converts multiple values into a single byte array (bytes). Used when sending data between contracts or as the message payload for Teleporter. Does not require any imports since it is part of the Solidity language.

A built-in Solidity function that converts a byte array back into its original typed values. The decoder must know both the types and their order as encoded - the encoding does not embed type information.
interchain-messaging.example: (string memory someString, uint someNumber) = abi.decode(message, (string, uint));

interchain-messaging.definition: The function on TeleporterMessenger that a source-chain dApp calls to initiate a cross-chain message. It takes a TeleporterMessageInput calldata struct as input and returns a uint256 message ID. The dApp itself does not need to implement ITeleporterMessenger.

The input struct passed to sendCrossChainMessage. Fields: destinationBlockchainID (bytes32), destinationAddress (address), feeInfo (TeleporterFeeInfo), requiredGasLimit (uint256), allowedRelayerAddresses (address[]), and message (bytes payload).

A bytes32 field in TeleporterMessageInput specifying the blockchain ID (in hex) of the destination chain. This is NOT the EVM chain ID; it is the P-Chain blockchain ID, which is the transaction ID of the transaction that created that blockchain on the P-Chain.

The amount of gas the delivery of an ICM message requires. If the relayer provides at least the required gas, the message is considered delivered whether or not its execution succeeds, so the relayer can claim their fee reward.

An address array in TeleporterMessageInput specifying which relayers are permitted to deliver the message. An empty array means any relayer can deliver it (open delivery, the default). Non-empty restricts delivery to listed relayer reward addresses.

A struct containing the ERC-20 contract address for the fee token and the amount of tokens to be paid as a relayer incentive. Setting feeTokenAddress to address(0) with amount 0 means no fee is offered.

The function has three parameters: originChainID (bytes32, the source blockchain ID), originSenderAddress (address, who called sendCrossChainMessage on the origin L1), and message (bytes calldata, the encoded payload from the sender).

A receiver contract must reject calls that do not come from the TeleporterMessenger. The standard pattern checks msg.sender against the stored TeleporterMessenger address and reverts with Unauthorized() otherwise.

The TeleporterMessenger contract is non-upgradable - once a version is deployed it cannot be changed. This prevents introducing bugs or vulnerabilities into deployed contracts but means new versions must be deployed as new contracts.

A contract that keeps track of TeleporterMessenger contract versions, allowing cross-L1 dApps to request the latest or a specific version. Internally it maintains a mapping of version numbers to contract addresses, with each registry independent per chain.

Version 0 in TeleporterRegistry is invalid and indicates that no TeleporterMessenger contract has been registered yet. Calling getAddressFromVersion(0) reverts with "TeleporterRegistry: zero version".

A view function on TeleporterRegistry that returns the ITeleporterMessenger registered at the highest version number (latestVersion). Allows dApps that use the registry to automatically benefit from protocol upgrades without redeployment.

Each TeleporterRegistry's mapping of version to contract address is independent of registries on other blockchains. Chains can decide their own registry entries, so version 4 on chain A need not equal version 4 on chain B.

Three core components: TeleporterMessenger (handles sending/receiving cross-chain messages), TeleporterRegistry (manages messenger versions, takes the messenger address as a constructor argument), and Relayer (off-chain service that picks up messages and delivers them).

An off-chain service that monitors source chains for outgoing warp messages, aggregates BLS signatures from validators, constructs a signed warp message once the threshold is met, and submits a transaction on the destination chain to deliver the message.

A managed relayer offered through Builders Hub Console that requires no infrastructure and is suitable for testing. The hosted relayer automatically shuts down after 3 days; self-hosted Docker relayers are an alternative for longer-running scenarios.

A single relayer instance can handle communication in both directions between chains. Enabled by adding both chains to both the source-blockchains and destination-blockchains arrays and ensuring the relayer account has sufficient funds on both chains for transaction fees.

Open delivery (allowedRelayerAddresses empty array) lets any relayer deliver the message - the default and most common approach. Restricted delivery requires listing specific relayer reward addresses, useful when running your own relayer or for privacy-sensitive messages.

A field in the source-blockchain message-contracts settings that specifies the address rewarded if an L1 incentivizes relayers to send messages on its behalf. This is the address of the wallet created for the relayer.

For each destination L1 the relayer needs a signing key: either account-private-key (a hex private key holding gas tokens on the destination) or kms-key-id paired with kms-aws-region (AWS KMS). Only one of these should be provided per destination.

Before sending, the user approves the cross-chain dApp as a spender on the ERC-20 fee token. On send, the dApp pulls the fee, approves TeleporterMessenger, which transfers tokens in, assigns a unique message ID, and records the fee info.

When TeleporterMessenger on the destination delivers a message, it generates a receipt with the messageID and the relayer's reward address. The receipt is sent back to the source chain, where TeleporterMessenger marks the messageID as received and credits the relayer's redeemable reward.

Relayers can either send receipts back to the source chain themselves or wait until an unrelated message is sent from the previous destination back to the previous source. Messages include up to 5 pending receipts attached to them.

A relayer delivers a message only when expected income exceeds cost. Cost = requiredGasLimit * gas_price_in_native_token * native_token_price. Income = Fee_Amount * ERC20_Price. Any Fee_Amount where Cost < Income is profitable, excluding hosting costs.

A general cross-Avalanche L1 communication protocol that makes all Avalanche blockchains natively interoperable. AWM provides signing and verification primitives in AvalancheGo that VMs use to sign outgoing messages and verify incoming messages. AWM is not EVM-specific.

A warp UnsignedMessage contains exactly four fields: NetworkID (Mainnet/Fuji/Local, prevents cross-network replay), SourceChainID (P-Chain blockchain ID, not EVM chain ID), SourceAddress (message sender), and Payload. Adding an aggregated signature produces a signed warp message.
Every Warp message is uniquely identified by its ID, which is the SHA256 hash of the serialized message containing NetworkID, SourceChainID, SourceAddress, and Payload.

Validators of the source L1 sign the unsigned warp message using their BLS private keys (whose public keys are registered on the P-Chain). The AWM Relayer aggregates these BLS signatures off-chain into a single aggregated signature before submitting to the destination.

Destination validators (1) query the P-Chain for the source L1 validator set with stake weights and BLS public keys, (2) verify the signers represent sufficient combined stake, (3) aggregate the BLS public keys of signing validators, (4) verify the BLS multi-signature, then (5) execute the message.

interchain-messaging.term: Why destination validators aggregate BLS public keys on-chain
interchain-messaging.definition: The BLS public key aggregation must be done by every destination validator rather than off-chain by the relayer. If the relayer aggregated public keys, there is no way to verify the aggregate corresponds to real validators - it could be fabricated. Aggregating on-chain from the P-Chain registry is the only secure approach.

Avalanche Warp Messaging uses the validator set of the source L1 as the trusted third party. Users already trust those validators by virtue of interacting with that L1. AWM does not introduce additional trust assumptions beyond what is already required to use the source chain.

The only liveness requirement is that some AWM Relayer is always available to perform signature aggregation and delivery of warp messages. Since relayers pay gas on the destination chain, the sender must provide incentives, or the receiving L1 may itself incentivize relayers.
