Sending messages between Avalanche L1s puts no load on the P-Chain or the Primary Network. The P-Chain merely functions as a registry of BLS public keys that destination L1 validators read from - there is no record of message data, signatures, or network communication on the P-Chain.

Each destination L1 arbitrarily sets the required share of combined source-L1 stake weight that must sign a warp message for it to be accepted. The threshold can vary per source chain - for example 50% for chain A and 90% for chain B.

The AWM Relayer learns about new outgoing warp messages on the source Avalanche L1 in two ways: Polling (periodically checking the source chain for new outgoing messages) or Notification (the relayer is triggered whenever a new outgoing message is detected by a node).

l1-native-tokenomics.definition: Ownership recorded on a public ledger and enforced by cryptography and consensus rather than legal registries. It is verifiable (anyone can audit balances and provenance), portable (assets move across apps and wallets with private keys), programmable (rights encode governance, access, royalties, staking, or fee distribution), and composable with DeFi and tooling through common standards.

Fungible tokens (e.g., ERC-20) track interchangeable units used for balances, payments, and governance power. Non-fungible tokens (e.g., ERC-721) track unique items and their provenance for collectibles, credentials, and rights. Hybrid multi-token standards such as ERC-1155 combine both models in a single contract.

l1-native-tokenomics.term: When to deploy your own L1 vs dApp on a public chain
l1-native-tokenomics.definition: Pick a custom L1 when the token must be native to the protocol (gas token, staking/slashing, fee burn/distribution, protocol-level issuance) and you need validator/permissioning guarantees. Pick a dApp with an ERC-20 on a public chain when utility is application-level (access, rewards, governance without base-fee mechanics) and you benefit from public-chain security and liquidity.

l1-native-tokenomics.term: L1 cost profile vs public chain
l1-native-tokenomics.definition: The primary cost of running an L1 is the infrastructure for the validator set. Once enough validators are running for the security target, operational costs are mostly fixed and independent of transaction count or complexity, giving predictable financial outlays as the application scales.

l1-native-tokenomics.term: Fee stability of a custom L1
l1-native-tokenomics.definition: On a public blockchain you are exposed to network-wide fee fluctuations. A custom Avalanche L1 isolates the fee to use the chain from activity on other chains, so high usage on the Primary Network does not affect fees on the L1 and gas costs stay predictable for budgeting.

Defines the smallest unit of an ERC-20 token and affects UX, pricing, rounding, and integrations. 6 decimals means the smallest unit is 0.000001; 18 decimals (common on EVM) means the smallest unit is 0.000000000000000001. ERC-20 exposes decimals() while native tokens inherit precision from the chain or protocol.

A fixed supply is an immutable cap set in the constructor or genesis. An elastic supply uses emissions, vesting, mint, or burn mechanisms to adjust supply over time, either through programmatic schedules or role-gated actions.

An ERC-20 extension that lets a holder approve a spender via an off-chain signature instead of an on-chain approve() call, enabling gasless approvals. It is an alternative to the standard approve/allowance/transferFrom flow.
l1-native-tokenomics.example: function permit(address owner, address spender, uint256 value, uint256 deadline, uint8 v, bytes32 r, bytes32 s) external;

l1-native-tokenomics.definition: Two patterns for restricting privileged token operations. Ownable centralizes authority in a single owner address. AccessControl is role-based, defining named roles like MINTER_ROLE and PAUSER_ROLE that can be granted to multiple addresses, commonly combined with timelocks and multisig admins for safer governance changes.

The primary cryptocurrency of an EVM blockchain (ETH on Ethereum, AVAX on Avalanche C-Chain, ALOT on Dexalot). It is used for value transfer, gas fees, staking/security in Proof-of-Stake networks, and sometimes governance. On Avalanche L1s the chain may designate different assets for gas and staking.
The unit used to price transaction fees, contract deployments, and other network operations. Native tokens are paid as gas so resources are allocated efficiently within the network.

l1-native-tokenomics.term: Core Wallet send flow on Fuji
l1-native-tokenomics.definition: To transfer AVAX between accounts, switch Core Wallet to the Fuji testnet, click Send, enter the recipient's address, enter the amount, review the transaction details, and click Send to confirm. The wallet must hold enough AVAX to cover the transfer amount and gas fees.

Ethereum Request for Comment number 20 is a fungible token standard on EVM-compatible chains. ERC-20 tokens are identical to one another and can be exchanged one-to-one. They are created and managed by smart contracts that conform to the standard, ensuring interoperability between tokens and decentralized applications.

Every ERC-20 token stores balances as a mapping from address to uint256 in contract storage. Addresses can belong to Externally Owned Accounts (EOAs) or smart contracts, both of which can hold and transfer ERC-20 tokens.

The standard interface includes name(), symbol(), decimals(), totalSupply(), balanceOf(address), transfer(address,uint256), transferFrom(address,address,uint256), approve(address,uint256), and allowance(address,address). Compliance lets any DApp interact with any token following the same rules.
l1-native-tokenomics.example: function transferFrom(address _from, address _to, uint256 _value) external returns (bool success);

l1-native-tokenomics.definition: approve() lets a token owner authorize a spender to withdraw up to a specified amount from their balance. transferFrom() then lets that spender (typically a smart contract) move tokens from the owner's account, enabling DeFi protocols to pull funds according to contract logic.

l1-native-tokenomics.term: ERC-20 vs native token: transfer logic
l1-native-tokenomics.definition: ERC-20 transfers are handled by contract functions transfer() and transferFrom(). Native token transfers are handled directly by EVM opcodes such as CALL and SELFDESTRUCT, operating on the EVM account balance rather than a contract storage mapping.

l1-native-tokenomics.term: Receiving native tokens in a function
l1-native-tokenomics.definition: Functions that receive ERC-20 tokens take the token address and amount as parameters and need no special modifier. Functions that receive the native token must be declared payable so the EVM allows value to be sent with the call.
l1-native-tokenomics.example: function deposit() external payable { /* msg.value holds the native amount */ }

l1-native-tokenomics.definition: Protocols cannot pull native tokens from a user's wallet using transferFrom() because there is no approve()/allowance mechanism at the protocol level. Native sends require the holder to push value via msg.value, which forces wrapping (e.g., WETH) for DeFi integration.

To supply ETH to Aave the user wraps native ETH into ERC-20 WETH, calls approve() granting Aave permission to spend the WETH, Aave then calls transferFrom() to pull the WETH from the user's wallet, and the user receives aTokens that accrue interest and serve as collateral for borrowing.

An ERC-20 representation of a native cryptocurrency (e.g., WAVAX, WETH). The native asset is locked in a smart contract and an equivalent amount of the wrapped token is minted, backed 1:1 by the underlying native asset. Wrapping makes the native asset compatible with dApps and protocols that require ERC-20 tokens.

deposit() is payable: the user sends native cryptocurrency to the wrapped token contract and the contract mints an equivalent amount of the wrapped ERC-20. withdraw(amount) burns the specified amount of wrapped tokens and returns the equivalent native cryptocurrency to the user.

l1-native-tokenomics.term: Independent tokenomics customizations on an Avalanche L1
l1-native-tokenomics.definition: Every Avalanche L1 can configure its own native token used for gas, define how transaction fees are calculated, specify the initial native token allocation in genesis, define if and who can mint more native tokens, and (for permissionless validation) define how a node can become a validator and what staking token is used.

l1-native-tokenomics.term: Custom native token vs existing ERC-20 as native
l1-native-tokenomics.definition: A custom native token gives complete control over the L1's gas token, custom tokenomics, business model flexibility (including a valueless gas token for gas-free UX), and isolated fee control. Using an existing ERC-20 (such as USDC) as the native token gives price stability and a familiar asset. The L1 Native Tokenomics course focuses on a custom native token; cross-chain native tokens are covered in the Cross-Chain L1 Native course.

l1-native-tokenomics.term: Native token vs staking token on an L1
l1-native-tokenomics.definition: On an Avalanche L1 the native token (used for paying gas) and the staking token (used for validator rewards and network security) do not have to be the same asset. Separating them allows different fee UX from validator economics.

Token symbols are typically 3-5 uppercase characters, distinct from existing popular tokens, and relevant to the project (e.g., ETH, AVAX, USDC, BTC). The symbol is how users identify the token in wallets, exchanges, and explorers.

In Solidity the native token is always referred to as "ether" in code regardless of the chain's actual token symbol. msg.value, address.balance, and unit denominations like 1 ether always refer to the native token of whichever chain the contract runs on.
l1-native-tokenomics.example: payable(recipient).transfer(1 ether); // moves one unit of the native token

l1-native-tokenomics.definition: The token name configured in the L1 genesis is purely cosmetic and is not recorded anywhere in the blockchain code except inside the wrapped native token contract. It is used for display purposes in wallets and explorers.

The creator of an Avalanche L1 can pre-allocate the native token to specific addresses during the chain genesis event. This initial token allocation determines how tokens are distributed among founders, early investors, the community, and reserve/treasury at network launch.

Initial native token allocations typically split between founders and the development team (incentive for continued work), early investors and backers (reward for early funding/risk), the community (via token sale, airdrop, or other distribution), and a reserve or treasury for ongoing development, marketing, and ecosystem growth managed by a DAO or designated entity.

Blockchains rely on a deterministic machine (the EVM) to calculate transitions from one state to the next. Every transaction modifies blockchain state in a predictable, reproducible way so all nodes reach consensus on the new state.

The EVM is a specification of behavior; each blockchain (Ethereum, Avalanche C-Chain, a custom L1) is an instance of that specification with its own configuration. Avalanche lets you configure your EVM instance with precompiles at the execution level, giving control over functions like native minting and fee configuration.

Solidity lacks the battle-tested libraries of Go, TypeScript, or Rust and is compiled to EVM opcodes designed for simple operations. Complex math like elliptic curve cryptography requires chaining many opcodes with expensive operations such as SSTORE (20,000+ gas), making advanced cryptography impractically expensive in pure Solidity. Precompiles are programs in efficient languages exposed at fixed addresses to bypass those limits.

A standard stateless precompile at address 0x01 that recovers an Ethereum address from an ECDSA signature. It is a basic precompile available on most EVM chains and performs computation without modifying blockchain state.
A standard stateless precompile at address 0x02 that computes the SHA-256 hash of input data. It is one of the basic cryptographic precompiles shared across EVM-compatible blockchains.

An Avalanche extension that injects state access through the PrecompileAccessibleState interface. Unlike stateless precompiles, stateful precompiles can read and modify account balances, read and write to contract storage, and access the full EVM state during execution, enabling native minting, fee configuration, and transaction/contract deployer permissioning at the protocol level.

Application Layer: where smart contracts and user applications run. Execution Layer: where the EVM processes transactions and executes code. Consensus Layer: where validators agree on the blockchain state. Precompiles live in the execution layer directly integrated into the EVM.

l1-native-tokenomics.term: Why precompiles live in the execution layer
l1-native-tokenomics.definition: Precompiles get direct state access (no interpreter overhead), execute as native code in Avalanche's client language Go instead of EVM bytecode (orders of magnitude faster), perform protocol-level operations regular contracts cannot (minting native tokens, configuring fees, validator permissions), and maintain a security boundary so user-deployed contracts cannot interfere with protocol operations.

A stateful precompile on Avalanche L1s that lets authorized addresses mint additional native tokens after genesis. It is deactivated by default, which produces a hard-capped supply. When enabled, it allows controlled inflation, valueless gas token experiences, and protocol flexibility as the ecosystem grows.

The Avalanche C-Chain uses AVAX as its gas token and there will only ever be 720 million AVAX. This is an example of a hard-capped native token supply, in contrast to L1s that activate the Native Minter Precompile to mint more native tokens over time.

The total amount of gas that can be used in a single block on the L1. It controls how much computation happens in one block, with the value representing the maximum amount of gas a single transaction can use (which would produce a single-transaction block).

Number of seconds the network aims to take to produce a new block. If blocks are produced faster than this rate, base fees are increased; if slower, base fees are decreased. It drives the dynamic fee adjustment loop.
The minimum base fee that any transaction can use and the cost of native token transfers on the L1. No matter how simple a transaction is, it will cost at least this minimum base fee.

The targeted amount of gas, including block gas cost, to consume within a rolling 10-second window. The base fee adjusts up or down based on whether realized usage exceeds or falls short of this target.

Controls how rapidly the base fee adjusts when the parent block consumed more or less gas than its target. A larger value makes the base fee change more gradually; a smaller value makes it change more rapidly.

Determines the block gas change rate relative to the targetBlockRate. If the parent block is produced at the target block rate, the block gas cost stays the same; if produced slower than the target, the block gas cost decreases by this step.

An initial period at the start of a vesting schedule during which no tokens are released to the beneficiary. After the cliff is reached, tokens begin to vest according to the schedule (linear, graded, or cliff vesting).

In a linear vesting schedule the vested amount grows uniformly with elapsed time: once past the cliff, vestedAmount = totalTokens * (block.timestamp - start) / duration. After start + duration the full totalTokens is vested.

A mathematical formula that defines the relationship between a token's price and its supply, enabling automated price discovery and liquidity without traditional market makers. As supply increases via purchases the price per token rises along the curve P(S); selling decreases supply and lowers the price. Common shapes include linear P(S)=a*S+b and exponential P(S)=e^(k*S).

A governance mechanism where the cost of votes for a single participant grows as the square of the number of votes cast. 1 vote costs 1 credit, 2 votes cost 4, 3 votes cost 9, 4 votes cost 16. This discourages any single participant from dominating outcomes and balances influence between large and small token holders, though it is vulnerable to Sybil attacks without identity controls.

native-token-bridge.definition: Deployed on the source chain (e.g., Fuji C-Chain), ERC20TokenHome locks an ERC-20 token (such as USDC) when users bridge to a remote L1, and releases the locked ERC-20 when users bridge back.

Deployed on the destination L1, NativeTokenRemote calls the Native Minter precompile to mint native tokens when receiving bridge messages, and burns native tokens when users bridge back to the source chain.

The Native Minter precompile, a built-in Avalanche L1 system contract that allows authorized contracts to mint native tokens, lives at the fixed address 0x0200000000000000000000000000000000000001 and must be configured in the chain's genesis.
The official Circle USDC token on the Fuji C-Chain testnet uses the contract address 0x5425890298aed601595a70AB815c96711a31Bc65 with 6 decimals, and testnet USDC is dispensed by the Circle Faucet.

To bridge tokens into an L1 so they become the native gas token, the L1's genesis must enable the Native Minter precompile; without it, the NativeTokenRemote contract cannot mint native tokens on that L1.

The initial reserve imbalance, set when deploying NativeTokenRemote, represents the amount of native tokens that already exist on the destination L1 (such as genesis allocations) that are not yet backed by tokens locked in the home contract.

Native-token bridges (NativeTokenRemote) require full collateralization of the initial reserve imbalance on the source chain before minting on the destination L1 is enabled; until collateral is fully deposited, minting is blocked.
The Circle Faucet dispenses 1 USDC per request to a wallet address on Avalanche Fuji and rate-limits the same address to one request every 2 hours.

The Teleporter Messenger is the contract deployed on each L1 that handles sending and receiving cross-chain Interchain Messaging (ICM) messages; ICTT bridge contracts depend on it to deliver bridge requests.

The Teleporter Registry tracks Teleporter versions on an L1 and is required by all ICTT contracts (such as ERC20TokenHome, NativeTokenHome, ERC20TokenRemote, NativeTokenRemote) so they can resolve the current Teleporter Messenger address.

A relayer is an off-chain process that observes Teleporter Messenger events on a source chain, signs the message with BLS, and delivers it to the destination chain; without a running relayer, ICM messages are created but never delivered and bridge transfers appear stuck.

BuilderHub managed testnet relayers are free, require no Docker, and automatically shut down after 3 days; for production or longer testing, a self-hosted relayer (typically via Docker) is required instead.

A self-hosted ICM relayer runs in Docker, requires the operator to fund the generated relayer address with gas on both source and destination chains, save the configuration locally, and run the provided Docker command to start the process.

In Chapter 1, USDC on Fuji C-Chain is locked by ERC20TokenHome, an ICM message is relayed to the L1, and NativeTokenRemote calls the Native Minter precompile to mint native gas tokens; each minted native token is backed 1:1 by USDC locked on Fuji.

In Chapter 2, the L1's native token is wrapped and locked by NativeTokenHome, an ICM message is relayed to C-Chain, and ERC20TokenRemote mints an ERC-20 representation; this direction exports the L1's native token to C-Chain DeFi as a tradable ERC-20.

In Chapter 3, native AVAX on Fuji C-Chain is wrapped to WAVAX and locked by NativeTokenHome, an ICM message is relayed to the L1, and NativeTokenRemote calls the Native Minter precompile so AVAX becomes the native gas token of the L1.

Chapter 1 (ERC-20 to Native) and Chapter 3 (Native to Native) require the Native Minter precompile because the destination L1 mints native tokens; Chapter 2 (Native to ERC-20) does not require Native Minter because the destination contract mints an ERC-20 instead.

NativeTokenHome locks an ERC-20 representation rather than the raw native gas token, so users deposit native tokens and the contract wraps them into the L1's wrapped token (e.g., WAVAX, WTEST) before locking; on withdrawal, the wrapped tokens are unwrapped back to native.

WAVAX is the ERC-20 wrapper for native AVAX on Fuji C-Chain; NativeTokenHome on Fuji uses an already-deployed WAVAX contract (auto-detected by the toolbox) to lock AVAX as WAVAX when bridging to an L1.

The wrapped native ERC-20 (such as WAVAX or WTEST) supports deposit() to wrap native tokens into wrapped tokens, withdraw(amount) to burn wrapped tokens and receive native back, balanceOf(address) for balances, plus standard ERC-20 transfer, approve, and transferFrom.
native-token-bridge.example: interface IWrappedNative { function deposit() external payable; function withdraw(uint256 amount) external; }

native-token-bridge.definition: ERC20TokenRemote is itself an ERC-20 token deployed on the destination chain that mints tokens when receiving bridge messages from its registered NativeTokenHome (or ERC20TokenHome) and burns tokens when users bridge back; users can transfer or trade it like any ERC-20.

Registration creates a bidirectional, secure link between a home contract and a remote contract: the home will only accept bridge requests from registered remotes, preventing unauthorized remote contracts from claiming locked tokens; it is a one-time setup per remote.

Collateral for a native-token bridge is deposited on the source chain in the home contract's underlying ERC-20: USDC into ERC20TokenHome on Fuji (Chapter 1) or WAVAX into NativeTokenHome on Fuji (Chapter 3).

Native-to-ERC-20 bridges (Chapter 2) do not require collateralization; the home contract simply locks the wrapped native token on the source side and ERC20TokenRemote mints the ERC-20 on the destination side, with no reserve imbalance to back.

When deploying ERC20TokenHome, the operator selects 'ERC-20 Token' as the Transferrer Type, supplies the underlying ERC-20 contract address (e.g., USDC at 0x5425890298aed601595a70AB815c96711a31Bc65 on Fuji), and confirms the Teleporter Registry address is configured.

When deploying NativeTokenHome, the operator selects 'Native Token' as the Transferrer Type, supplies the Wrapped Token address (e.g., WAVAX on Fuji or WTEST on a custom L1), and confirms the Teleporter Registry address.

Deploying NativeTokenRemote on the destination L1 requires the Teleporter Registry address on the L1, the source chain selection (e.g., Fuji C-Chain), the home contract address on the source chain, and an initialReserveImbalance value (0 if no pre-existing native supply).

Deploying ERC20TokenRemote on the destination (typically Fuji C-Chain) requires selecting the source L1, the NativeTokenHome address on the source L1, a token name, symbol, and decimals matching the source native token (usually 18).

native-token-bridge.term: Fuji C-Chain ID for ICTT tooling
native-token-bridge.definition: Fuji C-Chain has EVM chain ID 43113, which the BuilderHub toolbox enforces (via enforceChainId={43113}) when deploying ERC20TokenHome, ERC20TokenRemote, or sending test bridge transfers on the Fuji side.

Converting a Subnet into a sovereign L1 (via the ConvertSubnetToL1 toolbox flow) is an irreversible P-Chain operation; the Subnet ID being converted must be verified carefully before executing the transaction.

Before converting a Subnet to a sovereign L1, the operator must first create the chain (Create Chain tool), then launch a BuilderHub hosted node for it, and wait until the node is healthy and the chain is producing blocks.

After creating an L1 with the Create Chain tool, the operator must save the Subnet ID, Blockchain ID, and Chain ID; these identifiers are referenced by relayer configurations, ICM contracts, and bridge deployment tools throughout the course.

native-token-bridge.term: Use case for ERC-20 to native bridging
native-token-bridge.definition: An ERC-20 to native bridge is ideal when an L1 wants an established stablecoin (such as USDC) as its gas currency, needs predictable gas costs without a volatile native token, or targets enterprise or regulated environments that prefer fiat-pegged tokens.

native-token-bridge.term: Use case for native to ERC-20 bridging
native-token-bridge.definition: A native to ERC-20 bridge lets users export an L1's native token to C-Chain as a tradable ERC-20 so it can be used in C-Chain DEXs and DeFi protocols, or bridged onward to other Avalanche L1s.

native-token-bridge.term: Use case for native to native bridging
native-token-bridge.definition: A native to native (AVAX to L1 native) bridge is ideal when an L1 wants to use AVAX as its gas currency, build inside the broader Avalanche ecosystem, or inherit shared security and value alignment from AVAX staking.

Once a NativeTokenRemote is both registered with the home contract and fully collateralized on the source chain, the source token can be bridged into the L1 as native tokens and L1 native tokens can be burned to release the source token back to the source chain.

native-token-bridge.term: Token Backing in ERC-20 to native
native-token-bridge.definition: In Chapter 1's ERC-20 to native bridge, every native token minted on the L1 is backed 1:1 by USDC locked in the ERC20TokenHome contract on Fuji C-Chain, ensuring native supply can always be redeemed for USDC.

native-token-bridge.term: Token Backing in native to native
native-token-bridge.definition: In Chapter 3's native to native bridge, every native token minted on the L1 is backed 1:1 by WAVAX (wrapped AVAX) locked in the NativeTokenHome contract on Fuji C-Chain.

native-token-bridge.term: Bridge back: native to USDC flow
native-token-bridge.definition: To bridge native tokens back to USDC, the user switches to their L1 and uses the Token Transfer tool with NativeTokenRemote as source and ERC20TokenHome as destination; native tokens are burned on the L1 and USDC is released from the home contract on Fuji.

native-token-bridge.term: Bridge back: native to AVAX flow
native-token-bridge.definition: To bridge native L1 tokens back to AVAX, the user switches to their L1 and uses the Token Transfer tool with reversed source/destination so native tokens are burned and AVAX is released (unwrapped from WAVAX) by NativeTokenHome on Fuji.

native-token-bridge.term: Bridge back: ERC-20 to L1 native flow
native-token-bridge.definition: To bridge ERC20TokenRemote tokens back to the L1 as native, the user switches to C-Chain and uses the Token Transfer tool with reversed source/destination; the ERC-20 is burned on C-Chain and native tokens are released by NativeTokenHome on the L1.

Native gas tokens are not ERC-20-compatible, so NativeTokenHome cannot lock them directly; a wrapped ERC-20 (deposit native, receive wrapped; withdraw wrapped, receive native) lets the home contract custody value through standard ERC-20 transfer and approval mechanics.

The Token Transfer (TestSend) tool requires the operator to select the source bridge contract (home or remote), the destination bridge contract (the registered counterpart), and the amount of tokens to bridge in a cross-chain transfer.

If no relayer is running between the source and destination chains, Teleporter Messenger emits the cross-chain message on the source chain but no entity delivers it; the bridge transfer stays pending indefinitely and tokens remain locked until a relayer is started.

native-token-bridge.term: Stablecoin gas vs AVAX gas trade-off
native-token-bridge.definition: Using USDC as the native gas token yields predictable, fiat-stable gas costs but ties an L1 to an external issuer; using AVAX as the native gas token aligns the L1 with the broader Avalanche ecosystem and shared security via AVAX staking but inherits AVAX price volatility.

native-token-bridge.term: Contracts: who calls the Native Minter
native-token-bridge.definition: Only NativeTokenRemote calls the Native Minter precompile; it does so when receiving an ICM bridge message that authorizes minting native tokens on the destination L1. ERC20TokenRemote does not call the Native Minter because it mints an ERC-20 instead.

Chapter 1 source token is USDC (ERC-20) on Fuji; Chapter 2 source token is the L1's custom native token (e.g., TEST); Chapter 3 source token is AVAX (native) on Fuji. Each chapter wraps native sources before locking when applicable.

Chapter 1 uses ERC20TokenHome on Fuji (locks USDC). Chapter 2 uses NativeTokenHome on the L1 (locks wrapped WTEST). Chapter 3 uses NativeTokenHome on Fuji (locks WAVAX). The home contract always sits on the source chain.

Chapter 1 uses NativeTokenRemote on the L1 (mints native via Native Minter). Chapter 2 uses ERC20TokenRemote on C-Chain (mints ERC-20). Chapter 3 uses NativeTokenRemote on the L1 (mints native via Native Minter). The remote always sits on the destination chain.

native-token-bridge.term: L1 with no genesis supply collateral
native-token-bridge.definition: If an L1 has no pre-existing native token supply (initialReserveImbalance set to 0 during NativeTokenRemote deployment), no collateral deposit is needed and the bridge can begin minting once registration completes; the toolbox displays the required collateral amount when it is non-zero.

permissioned-l1s.definition: The Platform Chain is the registry of every validator in the Avalanche network, including Primary Network (X/P/C) validators and all L1 / legacy Subnet validators. Each validator is registered with a unique node ID, public key, and stake weight, mapped to the blockchain it validates. The P-Chain runs the platformvm virtual machine and is the single source of truth for validator state. It is not EVM-based, so interacting with it requires a compatible wallet such as Core.

Two P-Chain transactions issued to create a new L1 blockchain on Avalanche. CreateSubnetTx creates a Subnet identified by the transaction hash, and CreateChainTx adds a blockchain to that Subnet. Together they register the new chain on the P-Chain before any conversion to an L1.

L1 validators sync only the P-Chain rather than the entire Primary Network, so they always have the latest view of every validator set in the Avalanche network but do not participate in P-Chain consensus. This reduces hardware requirements compared to legacy Subnet validators that had to sync X-, P-, and C-Chains.

A Subnet/L1 is a validator set; a chain is a single blockchain validated by exactly one Subnet. While one Subnet can technically validate multiple chains (as the Primary Network does for X, P, and C), in production the relationship is almost always 1:1 for simpler management and resource isolation.

Validators of a legacy Subnet must be Primary Network validators with the 2,000 AVAX stake, must sync and validate the entire Primary Network (X, P, and C chains), and the validator set is constrained to existing Primary Network participants.

Post-Etna L1 validators do not stake 2,000 AVAX; instead they pay a continuous fee of approximately 1.33 AVAX per month. The fee adjusts based on network usage similar to a pay-as-you-go cloud service.

Interchain Messaging is the foundation that lets the Validator Manager Contract talk to the P-Chain. The VMC has direct access to the Warp precompile, so it can build and verify ICM messages natively for validator operations without a third-party relayer.

Unlike typical cross-chain apps where a relayer monitors messages, collects signatures, and submits them, validator management has the user directly submit transactions, sign, and manage the message flow between the L1 and the P-Chain. No third-party relayer service is involved.

Validator-management ICM messages are secured by BLS signatures. The L1's validators collectively sign, proving the validator set agrees on the operation, and the user includes these aggregated signatures when submitting to the P-Chain so the destination can verify origin and consent.

When users call a proxy contract, it forwards calls to the implementation using the EVM delegatecall opcode, executing the implementation's code in the proxy's own storage context. This is what lets logic be upgraded while state remains at the proxy address.

An OpenZeppelin pattern with strict separation between admin and user interactions: admin calls are handled by the proxy itself, user calls are delegated to the implementation. This prevents function selector clashes between the proxy and the implementation.

A proxy pattern that moves the upgrade logic into the implementation contract itself. More gas-efficient than the Transparent Proxy but requires careful implementation to avoid accidentally locking upgrades.

Many proxy instances point to a single beacon contract that stores the implementation address. Upgrading the beacon updates every linked proxy simultaneously, useful when running many identical contract instances.

A risk of proxy patterns where a proxy and its implementation expose functions with the same 4-byte selector, causing unexpected dispatch and potential vulnerabilities. The Transparent Proxy pattern specifically guards against this by routing admin selectors only at the proxy level.

A permissioned private (walled-garden) blockchain restricts RPC access so only authorized participants can read or write. A permissioned public blockchain has public read access and public endpoints, but only authorized entities can submit transactions or deploy contracts.

Access privacy hides the blockchain itself behind authorized RPC endpoints (a walled garden). Data privacy keeps the blockchain publicly accessible but encrypts transaction contents using techniques like FHE or zero-knowledge proofs. Most private blockchains rely on access privacy.

A sybil-protection mechanism that centralizes the power to decide who can validate the network in one account (EOA or multi-sig). Validators are chosen by identity and reputation rather than stake or hashpower, making PoA suitable for permissioned and regulated environments.

Sybil-protection mechanisms (PoA, PoW, PoS) determine who can participate in validation. Consensus mechanisms (Nakamoto, Snowman) determine how those validators agree on state. PoA decides who validates, then those validators use a consensus algorithm to finalize blocks.

Validators in Avalanche PoA can have different weights: higher weight means more influence in consensus, and the admin can adjust weights based on performance or trust. This enables flexible governance inside a permissioned framework.

Avalanche Community Proposal that reinvented Subnets. It introduced new P-Chain transaction types, Warp message formats, and the protocol-level mechanism for L1s to manage their own validator sets, along with a continuous-fee model that replaced the 2,000 AVAX stake.

Avalanche Community Proposal that standardizes how a Solidity contract should implement validator management on top of ACP-77. It defines the abstract ACP99Manager interface with initializeValidatorSet, completeValidatorRegistration, completeValidatorRemoval, and completeValidatorWeightUpdate.

The foundational abstract contract from ACP-99 that standardizes validator management for EVM-based L1s. It declares four essential functions: initializeValidatorSet, completeValidatorRegistration, completeValidatorRemoval, and completeValidatorWeightUpdate, all of which interact with the P-Chain via Warp messages.

The concrete ValidatorManager extends ACP99Manager and adds initiateValidatorRegistration, initiateValidatorRemoval, and initiateValidatorWeightUpdate, plus the inherited complete* functions. All of these state-changing functions are protected by the onlyOwner modifier.

Every validator lifecycle operation (registration, removal, weight update) has an initiate phase on the L1 that emits a Warp message, then a complete phase on the L1 that verifies the P-Chain response and finalizes state. Anyone can call the complete phase with the signed message.

A Validator Manager Contract can be deployed in three places: on the L1 it manages (default, self-contained), on the Avalanche C-Chain (failsafe, better multi-sig tooling), or on a separate management L1. Once deployed, the VMC's chain cannot be changed without redeploying.

Deploying the VMC on the C-Chain gives a failsafe if the L1 goes down, leverages the C-Chain's high availability, integrates better with existing DeFi and Safe/Ash multi-sig tooling, and is generally preferred for PoS plans where permissionless onboarding matters.

In an uncustomized Subnet-EVM genesis, the Subnet owner address is allocated 0xd3c21bcecceda1000000 wei, equal to 1,000,000 native tokens (1e6 × 1e18), useful as a funded deployer/faucet account on the new L1.

The TransparentUpgradeableProxy bytecode is pre-deployed at the well-known address 0xfacade0000000000000000000000000000000000 in every Subnet-EVM genesis. Its implementation slot points to a placeholder 0x1212...12 and its admin slot points to 0xdad...0.

The ProxyAdmin pre-deployed at 0xdad0000000000000000000000000000000000000 in genesis. It owns and manages upgrades of the TransparentUpgradeableProxy at 0xfacade...0 by holding the right to call upgradeToAndCall.

Standardized storage slots used by upgradeable proxies. In the Subnet-EVM genesis predeploy, slot 0x3608...bbc holds the implementation address and slot 0xb531...103 holds the admin address, avoiding collisions with implementation storage layout.

permissioned-l1s.term: Why pre-deploy the proxy in genesis
permissioned-l1s.definition: Three reasons: (1) ConvertSubnetToL1Tx requires a known VMC address as a parameter, so 0xfacade... must exist before conversion; (2) P-Chain transactions have a size limit, and the small proxy bytecode preserves space; (3) the proxy enables upgrading the implementation later without a network upgrade.

The ConvertSubnetToL1Tx struct on the P-Chain carries an Address field of type types.JSONByteSlice that holds the Validator Manager (or its proxy) address. For chains created via the Developer Console this is the pre-deployed proxy at 0xfacade...
permissioned-l1s.example: type ConvertSubnetToL1Tx struct { Address types.JSONByteSlice `serialize:"true" json:"address"` }

permissioned-l1s.definition: Converting a Subnet to a sovereign L1 establishes the VMC as the authority for validator management on the chain and is a one-time, irreversible process. Once converted, the chain cannot revert to Subnet status.

ConvertSubnetToL1Tx requires the Subnet ID (from CreateSubnetTx hash), the Validator Manager Blockchain ID (where the VMC lives), the Validator Manager Address (proxy or contract), and the Initial Validators list that forms the L1's first validator set.

A separate Solidity library that the ValidatorManager links against. It packs validator registration messages for the P-Chain, unpacks responses, and handles weight updates and conversion messages. It must be deployed independently before the ValidatorManager because Solidity requires library bytecode to be linked at deploy time.

To point the genesis proxy at the real Validator Manager implementation, the deployer calls upgradeToAndCall on the ProxyAdmin at 0xdad..., passing the proxy address 0xfacade..., the new implementation address, and any initialization data. The proxy then delegatecalls into the new implementation.

The struct passed to initialize() on the ValidatorManager. It contains the admin address (initial owner of validator operations), the Subnet ID (locked after initialization), the churn period in seconds (must be <= 86400), and the maximum churn percentage (1-20% protocol max).

The ValidatorManager's initialize() function is protected by the initializer modifier from OpenZeppelin Upgradeable contracts, which guarantees the function can only be called once for the lifetime of the proxy.

A parameter in ValidatorManagerSettings that caps how much of the total validator weight can change inside one churn period. The protocol maximum is 20%; for example a setting of 20% means at most 1/5 of total weight can churn per period.

When initializing the validator set, the contract computes the conversion ID by hashing the encoded conversion data and checks it equals the conversion ID inside the P-Chain Warp message. This guarantees the validator data was not tampered with.

Used inside ConversionData to describe each starting validator. It contains the validator's P-Chain nodeID (bytes), a 48-byte blsPublicKey for signature verification, and a uint64 weight representing voting power.
permissioned-l1s.example: struct InitialValidator { bytes nodeID; bytes blsPublicKey; uint64 weight; }

permissioned-l1s.definition: Called by the VMC owner with the validator's 20-byte nodeID, 48-byte blsPublicKey, remainingBalanceOwner, disableOwner, and weight. The contract validates formats, builds a RegisterL1ValidatorMessage with a bounded expiry, computes a unique validationID, stores pending state, and sends a Warp message via the precompile.

The P-Chain transaction issued during validator registration after the L1 emits the RegisterL1ValidatorMessage. It registers the validator with the subnet and, on success, returns a signed L1ValidatorRegistrationMessage that the L1 uses to complete registration.

Anyone (not only the VMC owner) can call completeValidatorRegistration(messageIndex) with the signed P-Chain response. The contract verifies the Warp message, marks the validator Active, sets its start time, and emits CompletedValidatorRegistration.

Validator registrations default to a 24-hour expiry. If the completion phase is not executed before the expiry time the registration is invalid but stays in the pending list. Expired registrations must be cleaned up manually via the Remove Expired Validator Registration tool.

Called by the VMC owner with the validator's validationID and the new weight. The contract requires the validator to be Active, enforces churn constraints, increments the sent nonce, updates pending weight in storage, and emits a Warp message to the P-Chain.

Removal is encoded on the wire as a weight update to zero. initiateValidatorRemoval sets the validator status to PendingRemoved, sets the validation end time, and sends an L1ValidatorWeightMessage with weight=0 to the P-Chain via the Warp precompile.

The P-Chain returns an L1ValidatorRegistrationMessage with a Valid boolean. Upon successful removal processing, valid=false is returned, which completeValidatorRemoval checks before transitioning the validator to Completed status and emitting CompletedValidatorRemoval.

A P-Chain transaction that disables a validator directly without going through the VMC. The weight still counts toward the L1's total weight but the validator becomes inactive and its keys are removed from disk. Used for emergencies when the L1 or VMC is unreachable.

An optional intermediary contract that sits between a multi-sig wallet and the ValidatorManager. It is initialized with the multi-sig as owner and the VMC as validatorManager, then exposes simplified addValidator, removeValidator, and changeValidatorWeight functions that the multi-sig can call without building complex calldata.

permissionless-l1s.definition: A sybil protection mechanism that centralizes control over who can validate the network to one account (EoA or multi-sig), using real-world identity and reputation rather than economic stake. Validators receive no on-chain rewards and face no on-chain penalties.

A sybil protection mechanism that ties participation power to economic stake. Anyone meeting the minimum stake requirement can become a validator — there is no voting mechanism to elect validators, making it more permissionless and decentralized than Delegated Proof of Stake (DPoS).

permissionless-l1s.term: Minimum validator stake on Avalanche Mainnet
permissionless-l1s.definition: 2,000 AVAX is the minimum stake required to become a Primary Network validator on Avalanche Mainnet. Staking duration must be between 2 weeks and 1 year.

permissionless-l1s.term: Minimum delegator stake on Avalanche Mainnet
permissionless-l1s.definition: 25 AVAX is the minimum amount a token holder must delegate on Avalanche Mainnet. Delegators do not run infrastructure — they delegate their stake to existing validators and share in their rewards.

permissionless-l1s.term: Default uptime requirement for staking rewards
permissionless-l1s.definition: Validators must maintain 80% uptime by default to be eligible for staking rewards. Failing to meet the uptime threshold means the validator receives only their original stake back without any validation reward.

A validator's influence in consensus, equal to the sum of their own staked tokens plus all tokens delegated to them by others. Weight determines polling influence in Avalanche consensus and is proportional to stake.

The backbone of Avalanche's native interoperability that acts as a registry of all validators in the network, including Primary Network and L1 validators. It runs the platformvm and is not EVM-based, so it requires a compatible wallet like Core.

A Subnet (or L1) is a validator set, while a chain is an individual blockchain instance. One Subnet can technically validate multiple chains, but in production a 1:1 relationship is recommended for simpler management and resource isolation.

L1 validators form a sovereign validator set independent of the Primary Network, pay a continuous fee (~1.33 AVAX/month), and only sync the P-Chain. Subnet validators must also be Primary Network validators and sync the entire Primary Network (X, P, C).

The smart contract system that manages validator operations in permissioned and permissionless L1s. It follows a two-phase commit pattern (initiate → complete) for all validator changes and uses Avalanche Warp Messaging to coordinate with the P-Chain.

The Validator Manager Contract can be deployed on the L1 itself or on the Avalanche C-Chain. Deploy on L1 for self-contained PoA setups; deploy on C-Chain if planning a PoS transition or wanting operational robustness if the L1 is degraded. The VMC location cannot be moved after deployment.

An abstract contract that extends ValidatorManager by adding functions for permissionless validator registration and removal based on staked tokens. It supports uptime-based validation rewards and delegation. Concrete implementations are NativeTokenStakingManager and ERC20TokenStakingManager.

A concrete PoSValidatorManager implementation that allows permissionless addition and removal of validators who stake the L1's native token. Uses Solidity's payable keyword to accept native tokens directly via msg.value during initiateValidatorRegistration and initiateDelegatorRegistration.

A concrete PoSValidatorManager implementation used when validators stake a separate ERC20 token rather than the native token. It stores a reference to an IERC20Mintable token, requires approval, and uses safeTransferFrom/safeTransfer to lock and unlock stake. Exists because native tokens cannot be used with payable on all blockchains.
0x0200000000000000000000000000000000000001 is the protocol-level address of the INativeMinter precompile. NativeTokenStakingManager calls mintNativeCoin on this address to mint staking rewards.

permissionless-l1s.term: Native Minter precompile requirement for PoS
permissionless-l1s.definition: When using the native token for staking, the Native Minter precompile is mandatory because it enables minting of staking rewards for validators. The NativeTokenStakingManager contract must be granted admin or enabled privileges on the precompile or reward distribution will fail.

An optional but highly recommended precompile that automates reward distribution based on validator performance. Without it, transaction fees are burned rather than distributed as rewards, eliminating economic incentives beyond minimal fee revenue.

Avalanche L1s allow separating the staking token (used to secure the network via PoS) from the native gas token (used to pay transaction fees). Many networks use the same token for both, but Avalanche L1s give builders the flexibility to use different tokens.

If an L1 uses a low-liquidity token as its staking token, an attacker can take out a loan or use existing capital to purchase >51% of the supply, gain consensus control, halt or double-spend the network, then sell the tokens back. This is why staking tokens must have substantial market depth.

A mechanism that allows users to stake tokens to secure the network while maintaining liquidity. The user receives a staking derivative (liquid token) representing their staked assets, which can be traded or used in DeFi while continuing to earn staking rewards.

permissionless-l1s.term: Recursive leverage risk in liquid staking
permissionless-l1s.definition: A consensus risk where using liquid staking tokens as collateral to repeatedly borrow and stake leads to outsized influence over network consensus, especially on smaller chains. Mitigations include limiting LTV ratios, applying rate limits, and promoting validator decentralization.

Converting an L1 from PoA to PoS requires opening the Validator Manager's restricted functions to the public (so anyone meeting economic requirements can register) and integrating token economics via the Native Minter (mandatory for native-token staking) and optionally the Reward Manager.

The Validator Manager Contract function that establishes the initial validator set when converting a Subnet to an L1. It verifies P-Chain signed conversion data via Avalanche Warp Messaging, registers each validator with their weight and node ID, and marks the validator set as initialized — a permanent, one-time operation.

The Validator Manager pattern for all validator changes: an initiate function (e.g. initiateValidatorRegistration) sends a Warp message to the P-Chain, and a complete function (e.g. completeValidatorRegistration) finalizes the operation using the P-Chain's signed response.

A constraint on the Validator Manager Contract that prevents rapid validator set changes by limiting weight changes per period. Configured via churnPeriodSeconds (≤ 86400) and maximumChurnPercentage (1-20%) during initialize().

The mechanism by which the Validator Manager Contract communicates with the P-Chain. The VMC constructs and verifies Warp messages — for example RegisterL1ValidatorMessage and L1ValidatorWeightMessage — to coordinate validator registration, removal, and weight updates across chains.

permissionless-l1s.term: P-Chain transaction types for L1 management
permissionless-l1s.definition: CreateSubnetTx creates a new subnet for an L1, CreateChainTx creates a new blockchain within a subnet, RegisterL1ValidatorTx adds a validator, and SetL1ValidatorWeightTx updates a validator's weight (also used for removal by setting weight to 0).

The pre-deployed proxy address (0xfacade0000000000000000000000000000000000) used as the Validator Manager Address during subnet-to-L1 conversion. The proxy is upgraded to point to the newly deployed ValidatorManager implementation contract.

Converting a subnet to a sovereign L1 establishes blockchain sovereignty and transfers validator management authority from the P-Chain to the Validator Manager Contract. The conversion is irreversible — you cannot revert from L1 status back to subnet.

The base abstract contract that defines _lock(), _unlock(), and _reward() as virtual functions. Cannot be deployed directly — concrete child contracts like NativeTokenStakingManager must implement _reward() (which mints via the Native Minter) and provide token-specific lock/unlock logic.
permissionless-l1s.example:
// In StakingManager (abstract)
function _reward(address account, uint256 amount) internal virtual;

permissionless-l1s.definition: A reference implementation of the IRewardCalculator interface that calculates rewards based on validator uptime percentage (80% threshold) using a linear, non-compounding reward formula. The reward rate is specified in basis points at deployment, where 100 bips equals 1% APR.
The unit used to specify the staking reward rate when deploying ExampleRewardCalculator. 100 basis points equals 1% annual percentage rate (APR), so entering 500 sets a 5% APR for staking rewards.

The configuration struct passed to the Staking Manager's initialize() function. Fields include Minimum Stake Amount, Maximum Stake Amount, Minimum Stake Duration, Minimum Delegation Fee Bips, Maximum Stake Multiplier, Weight to Value Factor, and Reward Calculator Address.

A PoSValidatorManagerSettings parameter that defines the conversion ratio between validator weight and token value. It is used by valueToWeight() during registration and weightToValue() for display, allowing the contract to translate staked token amounts into consensus weights.

After deploying the Staking Manager, its address must be added as an Enabled address on the Native Minter precompile so it can mint reward tokens. Only addresses with Manager or Admin permissions on the Native Minter can enable other addresses.

permissionless-l1s.term: Transfer Validator Manager ownership to Staking Manager
permissionless-l1s.definition: The one-time, irreversible step that transfers ownership of the Validator Manager from an EOA to the NativeTokenStakingManager contract. After transfer, the onlyOwner functions (validator add/remove/weight) become permissionless — anyone can invoke them via the Staking Manager's public functions.

All PoS staking operations follow the same three phases: (1) Initiation on the L1 — call a Staking Manager function that locks tokens and emits a Warp message; (2) P-Chain Processing — aggregate signatures and submit to the P-Chain; (3) Completion on the L1 — call a completion function with the P-Chain's signed response.

The percentage fee, in basis points, that a validator charges delegators on their staking rewards. Set during initiateValidatorRegistration and fixed for that validation period — it cannot be changed after registration. For example, 1000 bips = 10% fee.
permissionless-l1s.example:
// Validator charging 10% delegation fee
uint16 delegationFeeBips = 1000;

permissionless-l1s.definition: The minimum time a validator must stake before they can initiate removal. Set during registration and also applies to delegators of that validator. Enforced as: canRemove = block.timestamp >= startTime + minStakeDuration.

Helper functions on the PoSValidatorManager. valueToWeight(tokenAmount) converts a staked token amount into a validator weight during registration. weightToValue(weight) converts weight back to tokens for display. The conversion is governed by Weight to Value Factor.

PoS validator registrations on Avalanche expire after 24 hours by default. If completeValidatorRegistration is not called within this window after initiation, the registration becomes invalid and must be cleaned up.

Anyone can call completeValidatorRegistration(messageIndex) on the Staking Manager with the P-Chain's signed L1ValidatorRegistrationMessage. The function verifies the Warp signature, activates the validator (status → Active), sets the start time, and emits CompletedValidatorRegistration.

When a delegator calls initiateDelegatorRegistration, the Staking Manager locks the delegator's tokens, computes the new total validator weight (existing + delegation), increments the validator's nonce, and sends a L1ValidatorWeightMessage to the P-Chain — not a registration message.

permissionless-l1s.term: Validator must be Active for delegation
permissionless-l1s.definition: A delegator can only delegate to validators with Active status. Once a validator has initiated removal (status PendingRemoved), no new delegations can be added to that validator.

PoS validator removal is encoded as a weight update to zero. initiateValidatorRemoval constructs an L1ValidatorWeightMessage with weight = 0 and the P-Chain responds with a signed L1ValidatorRegistrationMessage where valid = false.

When initiating removal, the Staking Manager can accept an uptime proof from the P-Chain. If provided, it is used to calculate rewards; if not, the latest known uptime is used. Higher uptime yields higher rewards, so providing a fresh proof is beneficial.

A separate Staking Manager function the validator owner must call after their validation period ends to retrieve accumulated delegation fees. Fees are not distributed inside completeValidatorRemoval in order to bound gas consumption.

On delegator removal, the total reward is split between delegator and validator using delegationFeeBips: delegatorReward = totalReward * (1 - delegationFeeBips / 10000) and validatorFee = totalReward * (delegationFeeBips / 10000). The validator's share accumulates and is claimed separately.

The reward.Calculator computes staking rewards from four inputs: staking duration (how long tokens were staked), stake amount (validator weight = own + delegated), current total token supply (affects rate), and network parameters (configured reward rates and caps).

permissionless-l1s.term: Commit vs Abort path for RewardValidatorTx
permissionless-l1s.definition: When a validator's end time is reached, the Proposal Executor runs a RewardValidatorTx as a proposal. On the commit path the validator receives stake + validation reward; on the abort path the validator receives only their stake, potential rewards are burned (supply decreased), and accumulated delegation fees are still returned.

blockchain-fundamentals.definition: A new kind of computer whose key advantage is decentralization: no single entity controls the system, control is distributed across many participants, and transactions are verified by consensus and recorded on an immutable ledger.

Blockchains are inherently less efficient than traditional centralized systems. The complexity of managing a decentralized network and slower transaction speeds are significant downsides versus centralized computing models.

A self-executing program stored on a blockchain that automatically executes when predetermined conditions are met. The terms of the agreement are written directly into code and operate without a trusted central authority.
A program that runs on a blockchain, often consisting of multiple smart contracts that together offer a full application experience. dApps can manage digital assets, run DeFi protocols, and more.

Blockchains enable secure, transparent transactions without intermediaries. Immutability ensures financial records are tamper-proof and auditable, which is the foundation of cryptocurrencies and decentralized finance (DeFi) platforms.

blockchain-fundamentals.term: Blockchain use case: Supply Chain Management
blockchain-fundamentals.definition: Blockchain provides an immutable record of a product's journey from origin to consumer, increasing transparency and reducing fraud by accurately tracking and verifying every step of the supply chain.
Blockchain's transparency and security ensure that votes are accurately counted and cannot be altered. This is used to maintain democratic integrity and build trust in electoral processes.

The current amount of money or assets held in a specific account at a given time. It reflects the total value after all credits (additions) and debits (subtractions) have been accounted for, indicating what the account holder has available.

The process of moving money or assets from one account to another. The balance of the sending account is reduced by the amount being transferred and the receiving account is increased by the same amount, keeping total value constant.

A transaction request that exceeds the available balance in the sending account. The system rejects it to prevent overdrafts and both sending and receiving balances remain unchanged, preserving financial-record integrity.

A comprehensive record-keeping system that documents all transactions. It maintains a detailed and chronological list capturing every transfer across accounts so that every action is accurately tracked and traceable.

A ledger where once a transaction is recorded it cannot be altered or deleted. The historical record remains intact and unchangeable, preserving original data even when errors or invalid transactions are recorded.
A ledger that operates by adding new transactions to the end of the record without altering previous entries. To reverse a transaction's effects, a new counteracting transaction must be appended.

A cryptographic mechanism that ensures the authenticity, integrity, and non-repudiation of digital messages or transactions. It acts as an electronic fingerprint that uniquely binds a transaction to its originator.
The property of a digital signature that confirms the identity of the transaction sender by proving the holder of the corresponding private key authorized the transaction.
The property of a digital signature ensuring the transaction data hasn't been altered. If any character of a signed transaction is modified, the signature becomes invalid.
The property of a digital signature that prevents the sender from denying they made the transaction, providing a mathematical proof of authorization tied to their private key.

A cryptographic scheme using a pair of mathematically related keys: a private key and a public key. Data signed with the private key can be verified using the public key, but the private key cannot be derived from the public key.

A secret key known only to the owner, used to sign transactions and which must be kept secure. Anyone with access to a private key can impersonate the owner and authorize transactions on their behalf.

A publicly shared key derived from the private key. Anyone can use it to verify signatures created by the corresponding private key, and it serves as the owner's address or identity on the blockchain.
Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm. A signature scheme that provides strong security with relatively small key sizes, making it efficient for blockchain applications.
Boneh-Lynn-Shacham signatures. A signature scheme that provides small sized signature aggregations, maintaining security while saving space when multiple signatures are needed.
The protocol that enables distributed network participants to agree on a single, authoritative version of the truth: the order and validity of transactions on the blockchain.
blockchain-fundamentals.example: Key objectives: Agreement, Validity, Termination, Integrity.

blockchain-fundamentals.definition: The scenario in which the same money or token is spent more than once. In decentralized blockchains, consensus determines which transaction is processed first; the second is rejected as invalid (insufficient balance).

Short for memory pool: a collection of unconfirmed transactions stored in each node's RAM. Each node maintains its own mempool, and transactions wait here until a miner or validator includes them in a block.

When multiple valid versions of the blockchain exist, nodes should accept the chain with the most accumulated weight as the authoritative chain. Longest means greatest cumulative weight, not necessarily the most blocks.

A temporary split where the blockchain has two competing valid versions, typically because two validators proposed valid blocks at nearly the same time and different nodes received different blocks first.
A valid block that is no longer part of the main chain after the longest chain rule resolves a fork. Its transactions return to the mempool to be included in future blocks.
The number of blocks added after the block containing a transaction. 1 confirmation means the transaction is in the latest block; more confirmations mean the transaction is deeper and more secure.
A finality model used by longest-chain systems where transactions are never 100% absolute final, but the probability of reversal becomes negligibly small after several confirmations.
A finality model where, once finalized, transactions are mathematically impossible to reverse. Used in some Proof of Stake systems with finality gadgets or BFT consensus.

An attack where an entity controls more than 50% of the network's validation power and can create a private chain that outpaces the honest chain, then release it so nodes switch via the longest chain rule and reverse honest transactions.
When a long chain is replaced by an even longer competing chain. Shallow reorgs of 1-2 blocks happen naturally due to network delays; deep reorgs of 6+ blocks are rare and usually indicate an attack.

A sequence number included in a transaction that prevents the same transaction from being processed multiple times. In account-based systems, nodes verify the nonce matches the expected sequence number for the sender.

After signing, the wallet sends the signed transaction to one or more peer nodes. Each node performs basic validation and forwards it to its peers via a peer-to-peer gossip protocol until the transaction propagates throughout the network.

An attack where a single entity creates multiple fake identities (Sybil nodes) to gain disproportionate influence or control over a network, potentially manipulating consensus, censoring transactions, or rewriting history.

Strategies designed to make it expensive or impractical for an attacker to create multiple identities. Influence in the network is tied to something scarce and difficult to fake: computation, stake, hardware, or personhood.

Consensus mechanisms achieve agreement on the state of the blockchain (order/validity of transactions). Sybil defense prevents a single entity from controlling multiple identities to gain disproportionate influence. Both are necessary.

A Sybil-defense scheme where identities must perform expensive computations. Creating fake miner identities gives no extra power; what matters is total computational power (hash rate), making Sybil attacks prohibitively expensive.

A Sybil-defense scheme that requires identities to stake valuable assets. Influence is proportional to stake, so an attacker would need to acquire and risk significant capital to create many influential identities.

The primary digital currency or cryptocurrency native to a blockchain running the Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM). It serves as the foundation for value transfer and network operation within its ecosystem (e.g., ETH on Ethereum, AVAX on Avalanche C-Chain).
Native tokens used to pay for transaction fees, contract deployments, and other network operations on an EVM blockchain. Gas ensures that network resources are allocated efficiently.

An Ethereum Request for Comment standard (proposal 20) defining fungible tokens on EVM-compatible chains. Tokens are identical to one another, exchangeable 1:1, and managed through smart contracts adhering to the standard.
An account controlled by a private key, as opposed to a smart contract account. Both EOAs and smart contracts can hold and transfer ERC-20 tokens.
Allows the owner of a token balance to approve another account (the spender) to withdraw up to a specified amount of tokens from the owner's balance, possibly across multiple withdrawals.
blockchain-fundamentals.example: function approve(address _spender, uint256 _value) external returns (bool success);

blockchain-fundamentals.definition: Facilitates the transfer of tokens from one account to another on behalf of the account owner, typically used when smart contracts execute token transfers according to their internal logic.
blockchain-fundamentals.example: function transferFrom(address _from, address _to, uint256 _value) external returns (bool success);

blockchain-fundamentals.definition: A blockchain asset that represents a native cryptocurrency in tokenized form, typically conforming to the ERC-20 standard. The native token is locked in a smart contract and an equivalent amount of the wrapped token is minted, backed 1:1.

The level of precision used to define a token's smallest unit, specified when the token contract is created. A token with 18 decimals has a smallest unit of 0.000000000000000001, while a token with 6 decimals has a smallest unit of 0.000001.

A decentralized computer that defines the application-level logic of a blockchain. It specifies the blockchain's state, state transition function, transactions, and the API through which users interact with the blockchain.

Avalanche's public test network. It mirrors Mainnet functionality but uses valueless tokens and non-production infrastructure, providing a safe, low-stakes environment for developers to build, experiment, and validate before Mainnet deployment.

encrypted-erc.definition: Any representation of value, rights, or property stored and transacted on a blockchain, including cryptocurrencies, stablecoins, in-game items, tokenized real estate, and certificates.
encrypted-erc.example: USDC stablecoin, FIFA+ Collect NFT, or a tokenized real-world asset on Avalanche C-Chain.

encrypted-erc.definition: The EVM fungible token standard where each unit is identical and interchangeable, commonly using 18 decimal places for microtransactions.
encrypted-erc.example:
function transfer(address to, uint amount) external returns (bool);
function balanceOf(address account) external view returns (uint256);

encrypted-erc.definition: The EVM non-fungible token standard where each token has a unique tokenId, supports metadata for traits and images, and provides immutable proof of ownership.
encrypted-erc.example:
function ownerOf(uint256 tokenId) external view returns (address);
function tokenURI(uint256 tokenId) external view returns (string memory);

encrypted-erc.definition: The EVM multi-token standard that supports fungible, non-fungible, and semi-fungible tokens within a single contract, with batch transfer support for gas efficiency.
encrypted-erc.example: function safeBatchTransferFrom(address from, address to, uint256[] ids, uint256[] amounts, bytes data) external;

encrypted-erc.definition: The Avalanche contract chain that is fully compatible with the Ethereum Virtual Machine and hosts most digital assets created and managed on Avalanche.
The Avalanche test network where developers test token logic, minting, burning, metadata integration, and batch transfers before deploying to C-Chain mainnet or an L1.
Wrapped AVAX, an ERC-20 token used for liquidity pools, lending markets, and DEX operations on Avalanche C-Chain.
The ERC-20 governance token powering governance and incentives in the Trader Joe protocol on Avalanche C-Chain.
The ERC-20 governance token for the Benqi lending and liquid staking platform on Avalanche.
A blockchain design where balances, transaction amounts, and asset ownership are publicly visible to anyone querying the chain. ERC-20, ERC-721, and ERC-1155 all operate on transparent ledgers.
Avalanche is pseudonymous, not anonymous: users are identified by a public address rather than a real name, but once an address is linked to an identity all activity from it becomes traceable.
encrypted-erc.example: Address 0x1234...abcd is pseudonymous until a KYC exchange deposit links it to a real person.

encrypted-erc.definition: The property where once a blockchain address is tied to an identity through KYC, exchange deposits, or off-chain information, all past and future activity from that address can be analyzed.
Inferring information about an address owner by analyzing on-chain patterns such as dApps used, tokens traded, or NFTs purchased.
Know Your Customer / Anti-Money Laundering regulations that require identifying and verifying participants to prevent illegal activity, enforced in finance, healthcare, gaming, and government.

The ability to reveal transaction details only to authorized parties while keeping them hidden from the public, a compliance feature missing from standard ERC-20, ERC-721, and ERC-1155 implementations.
A privacy model where only authorized entities can see sensitive transaction details, enabling enterprise and regulated market adoption while preserving confidentiality from the public.
Regulators, tax authorities, or compliance officers can review specific transactions without making them public, via revocable permissions that can be updated without redeploying the token contract.
Avalanche's encrypted token standard that delivers encrypted balances, private transfer amounts, and compliance-ready auditability while remaining EVM-compatible on C-Chain, Fuji, and custom L1s.
Token balances are fully encrypted on-chain, visible only to the token holder and authorized auditors, preventing competitors and analysts from tracking holdings.
Transfer amounts are encrypted and not visible on public explorers like SnowTrace; observers can see that a transfer occurred but not how much was sent. Validated with zero-knowledge proofs.

A cryptographic proof that a transaction is valid without revealing its details, used in eERC to prove a sender has sufficient balance and the transfer respects protocol rules without disclosing amounts or balances.
An encryption scheme that allows operations on encrypted values without decrypting them, enabling eERC balance updates and validations while keeping amounts hidden on-chain.
A twisted Edwards elliptic curve optimized for zk-SNARKs, offering fast signature verification inside zero-knowledge circuits. Used in eERC for efficient key generation and proof validation.

A public-key encryption scheme adapted for elliptic curve cryptography over Baby Jubjub, used to encrypt token balances and transfer amounts while supporting homomorphic addition for encrypted balance updates.

A ZK-friendly hashing algorithm designed for use inside proof circuits, optimized for minimal gas cost and high efficiency in zk-SNARK verification. In eERC it is used for commitments and ciphertext integrity checks.
A component built into eERC that enables selective decryption of transactions for authorized auditors or regulators, with permissions that can be rotated or revoked without redeploying the contract.
The eERC on-chain contract that manages the list of authorized auditors and handles public keys for encryption and decryption permissions.
The eERC on-chain contract that stores all encrypted balances for token holders and updates balances through zk-SNARK validated operations.
The eERC on-chain contract that controls access and permissions for auditors and integrates with the Auditability Module.
Specialized zk circuits in eERC that verify transactions meet the protocol's rules without revealing transaction details, optimized for Baby Jubjub, Poseidon hashing, and ElGamal operations.
An eERC deployment mode where the token is fully private from creation, all balances and amounts are encrypted from the first mint, and total supply can optionally be hidden from the public.
An eERC deployment mode that wraps an existing ERC-20 token into an encrypted form, enabling private transfers while preserving the ability to unwrap it back to the original ERC-20.
An optional feature of eERC Standalone Mode that conceals the circulating supply of the token from the public, preventing outside insight into circulation. Optional in Converter Mode.
The default zk-SNARK proving system used by the official Avalanche eERC contracts, chosen for its fast verification, small proof size, and low gas cost on L1 and C-Chain deployments.

An alternative zk-SNARK proving system with universal setup and flexibility for new circuits; not supported by default in eERC and requires a custom build with modified Circom circuits and regenerated verifiers.
A zk proving system with no trusted setup and recursion support; not supported by default in eERC and requires major changes to the Circom circuits and verifier contracts to integrate.
To switch from Groth16 to PLONK or Halo2, a developer must modify the Circom circuits, regenerate the zk verifiers, and deploy new contract versions using those verifiers.
The circuit description language used to write the zk circuits behind eERC; modifying these circuits is required when changing the proving system or proof inputs.
The component used in eERC Converter Mode to wrap an existing ERC-20 token into an encrypted format during deployment.
The Converter Mode operation that locks ERC-20 tokens and mints equivalent encrypted eERC tokens (eERC20) to the depositor's encrypted balance.
The Converter Mode operation that burns encrypted eERC20 tokens and unlocks the underlying ERC-20 tokens back to the user's public balance.
The step in the backend converter flow that registers a user's wallet with the eERC system so it can hold encrypted balances and participate in private transfers.
The configuration step that assigns an authorized auditor in the Registrar Contract, granting decryption permissions for compliance scenarios.
A private transfer of eERC tokens between two registered users where the amount is encrypted with ElGamal over Baby Jubjub and validated by a zk-SNARK proof.
The Hardhat-based repository used in the academy walkthrough to deploy and interact with an eERC in Converter Mode on Fuji.
The HTTPS RPC endpoint used by Hardhat in the eERC backend converter setup to connect to the Avalanche Fuji C-Chain testnet.

Wallet integrates and encrypts the amount with ElGamal over Baby Jubjub, generates a Poseidon hash of the ciphertext, the user creates a zk-SNARK proof, the smart contract verifies the proof in a zk-friendly circuit, then encrypted balances in EncryptedUserBalances are updated and the transaction is recorded on Avalanche with encrypted values only.

encrypted-erc.term: On-chain visibility of an eERC transfer
encrypted-erc.definition: Block explorers show that a transfer occurred between two addresses, but they do not reveal the transferred amount, the updated balances, or the plaintext ciphertext contents.

Gas costs from proof verification require circuit optimization, auditor key rotation requires planning for secure revocation and re-assignment, and privacy settings must align with jurisdictional compliance requirements.

ERC-20 has no privacy technology and exposes all balances and transfer amounts; eERC uses zero-knowledge proofs (zk-SNARKs) combined with homomorphic encryption to keep balances and transfer amounts hidden while still verifiable.

nft-deployment.definition: A standard interface for non-fungible tokens (NFTs) on EVM chains, where each token is unique and has its own distinct value, unlike ERC-20 which defines interchangeable fungible tokens.
The property where each unit of an asset is identical and interchangeable. ERC-20 tokens are fungible (1 USDC equals 1 USDC); ERC-721 NFTs are non-fungible because each token is unique.

ERC-20 cannot represent non-fungible assets like an art collection because individual pieces vary in value. ERC-721 was introduced to handle digital art, collectibles, gaming items, and other unique assets.
Emitted whenever ownership of any NFT changes. Indexed by `_from`, `_to`, and `_tokenId`, allowing efficient off-chain indexing of ownership history.
nft-deployment.example: event Transfer(address indexed _from, address indexed _to, uint256 indexed _tokenId);

nft-deployment.definition: Emitted when the approved address for an NFT is changed or reaffirmed. Tracks single-token approvals for delegated transfers.
nft-deployment.example: event Approval(address indexed _owner, address indexed _approved, uint256 indexed _tokenId);
